<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"List any four economic benefits of Statistical Quality Control."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Reduction in rejection and rework cost** — SQC detects assignable causes early, preventing the production of defective items, thereby saving material, labor, and overhead.
2. **Economy in inspection** — SQC replaces costly 100% inspection with rational sample-based monitoring, substantially reducing inspection cost.
3. **Lower total cost of quality** — SQC emphasizes prevention over detection, minimizing the sum of prevention, appraisal, and failure costs.
4. **Better utilization of resources** — Stable processes run with less downtime, lower scrap, and higher machine efficiency.

*(1 mark for each valid point; ½ mark for naming, ½ mark for one-line explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"Discuss any three limitations of SQC."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Requires statistical knowledge and training** — Operating and interpreting control charts and sampling plans demands trained personnel, which may be lacking in small-scale industries.
2. **Only reduces, does not eliminate, variation** — SQC can address assignable causes, but chance (inherent) causes of variation cannot be removed, limiting the extent of quality improvement.
3. **Not suitable for small production runs** — Statistical methods need sufficient sample sizes; very small batches provide inadequate data for reliable inference.

*(1 mark each, with one-line justification.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper, Module 1 Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **benefits of Statistical Quality Control** under the heads: (i) Economic, (ii) Quality, and (iii) Managerial. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **limitations of SQC** with suitable examples. Why is SQC said to be a **supplement to, and not a substitute for, engineering judgment**? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Benefits of SQC (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Valuation Tip: Group your answer under **three clear heads** (Economic, Quality, Managerial) and list at least 2 benefits under each. Marks are awarded per *valid classification*.

**(i) Economic Benefits:**

- **Reduction in cost of poor quality:** SQC identifies assignable causes early, reducing rejection, rework, and scrap — directly lowering the *failure cost* component of the Cost of Quality.
- **Economy in inspection:** Statistical sampling replaces 100% inspection, cutting appraisal cost significantly.
- **Reduced downtime:** Stable processes mean fewer machine breakdowns and stoppages.

**(ii) Quality Benefits:**

- **Improved product uniformity:** By controlling variation, SQC produces consistent output that meets specifications.
- **Tighter tolerances:** Statistical understanding allows setting and achieving tighter specifications than ad-hoc methods.
- **Process capability assessment:** Indices like $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ quantify process performance against specification limits.

**(iii) Managerial / Organizational Benefits:**

- **Data-driven decision making:** Acceptance and rejection of lots are based on objective statistical evidence, not intuition.
- **Improved vendor–purchaser relations:** Acceptance sampling plans provide a fair, contractual basis for evaluating incoming material.
- **Promotes quality consciousness:** Workers, supervisors, and managers become more aware of variation and quality standards, fostering a culture of continuous improvement.

**Part (b) — Limitations of SQC (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> For full marks, students must **list** at least 4–5 limitations and **explain** them, then add a concluding philosophical remark on engineering judgment.

1. **Requires statistical training:** Operating personnel need familiarity with probability, distributions, and control chart interpretation.
2. **Only applicable where quality is measurable:** Qualitative attributes (e.g., colour, texture) cannot be directly subjected to SQC.
3. **Cannot eliminate chance causes:** SQC can detect and remove assignable causes, but inherent (random) variation remains.
4. **High initial investment:** Setting up a quality control laboratory, training staff, and installing measuring equipment involves substantial capital outlay.
5. **Not suited for small production runs:** Statistical inference requires adequate sample sizes; small batches offer insufficient data.
6. **Sampling risks:** Acceptance sampling inherently involves producer's risk ($\alpha$) and consumer's risk ($\beta$).
7. **Resistance from employees and management:** Workers may view SQC as surveillance; management may resist the long payback period.

**SQC as a Supplement, Not a Substitute for Engineering Judgment:**

SQC provides statistical signals and decision frameworks, but **interpreting** those signals in context — diagnosing root causes, deciding corrective actions, and predicting system behavior — requires **engineering expertise**. A point outside the control limit is a *symptom*; only an engineer can determine the *cure* (e.g., tool wear, material change, calibration drift). Therefore, SQC is a powerful tool that **augments** engineering judgment, never replaces it.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Retest Paper, Module 1 Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** "SQC minimizes the total cost of quality." Justify this statement with the help of a **mathematical derivation** of the optimal quality investment level. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Enumerate the **strategic and economic benefits** of SQC, and discuss **two major limitations** that constrain its adoption in small-scale industries. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Mathematical Derivation of Optimal Quality Cost (7 marks)**

The **total cost of quality** is:

$$
C_T = C_P + C_A + C_F
$$

where $C_P$ = prevention cost, $C_A$ = appraisal cost, $C_F$ = failure cost.

As the **quality investment level** $Q$ increases (more prevention + appraisal):

- $C_P$ and $C_A$ **increase** (linearly or quadratically),
- $C_F$ **decreases** (since fewer defects reach the customer).

A simple convex model:

$$
C_T(Q) = aQ^2 + \frac{b}{Q} + c
$$

where $a, b, c > 0$ are constants, $Q$ is the investment level.

**Step 1: Differentiate with respect to $Q$.**

$$
\frac{dC_T}{dQ} = 2aQ - \frac{b}{Q^2}
$$

**Step 2: Set the derivative to zero for the minimum.**

$$
2aQ - \frac{b}{Q^2} = 0
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $Q^*$.**

$$
2aQ^3 = b \quad \Longrightarrow \quad Q^* = \left( \frac{b}{2a} \right)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 4: Confirm it is a minimum using the second derivative test.**

$$
\frac{d^2 C_T}{dQ^2} = 2a + \frac{2b}{Q^3} > 0
$$

Since the second derivative is positive, $C_T$ is convex and $Q^*$ is indeed the minimum.

**Step 5: Economic interpretation.**

$$
\boxed{\ Q^* = \left( \frac{b}{2a} \right)^{1/3} \ \text{ gives the optimal quality investment level that minimizes } C_T. \ }
$$

This proves that SQC, by rationalizing prevention and appraisal, **minimizes the total cost of quality** — confirming the statement.

**Part (b) — Strategic & Economic Benefits and Small-Scale Limitations (7 marks)**

**Strategic and Economic Benefits:**

- **Competitive advantage:** Lower defect rates → fewer customer complaints → higher market share.
- **Improved profitability:** Reduced scrap, rework, and warranty costs directly enhance margins.
- **Customer satisfaction:** Uniform quality builds brand trust and loyalty.
- **Process standardization:** SQC data becomes the benchmark for continuous improvement initiatives.
- **Regulatory compliance:** Statistical documentation helps meet ISO 9001, FDA, and other quality standards.

**Limitations in Small-Scale Industries:**

1. **High initial investment:** Small firms often lack capital to set up laboratories, hire statisticians, and install measuring instruments — making SQC adoption financially prohibitive.

2. **Lack of trained personnel:** Small firms typically employ semi-skilled workers who may not be trained in statistical techniques, leading to misinterpretation of control charts and sampling plans.

3. **Small batch sizes:** Small-scale production often involves short runs of diverse products, making statistical sampling unreliable due to insufficient sample sizes.

4. **Resistance to change:** Family-run small businesses may resist formal quality systems, preferring traditional, experience-based quality checks.

5. **Limited data infrastructure:** Small firms often lack ERP/MIS systems to record, store, and analyze quality data over time.

**Conclusion:** SQC yields maximum benefit in **repetitive, large-scale production** environments; in small-scale or job-shop contexts, its application is **constrained** by economic and operational limitations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing "SQC has limitations" without **explaining** them. *(Always pair each limitation with a one-line justification.)*
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **Quality Control (QC)** with **Quality Assurance (QA)** in definitions.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Listing only benefits or only limitations in a "Discuss both" question — KTU awards marks for **both** sides.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting units or justification in the **mathematical derivation** (Step-by-step working carries 5 of 7 marks).
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Not concluding with an **engineering interpretation** — KTU's OBE framework rewards applied thinking.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Board Prep)**

- ✅ **SQC = Statistics + Engineering + Economics** — never treat it as pure math.
- ✅ **Benefits are classified** into Economic, Quality, Managerial, and Strategic — *use these four heads in answers.*
- ✅ **Limitations arise from** technical, economic, and operational sources.
- ✅ **Sampling risks** ($\alpha$ and $\beta$) are *inherent* to SQC, not flaws.
- ✅ **SQC minimizes, not eliminates, variation** — the Juran cost curve justifies this.
- ✅ **SQC complements, not replaces, engineering judgment.**
- ✅ **Most effective in repetitive mass production**, least in job-shop or one-off production.
- ✅ **Cost of Quality:** $C_T = C_P + C_A + C_F$ — minimum occurs at $Q^* = (b/2a)^{1/3}$.
- ✅ **Process capability indices** $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ are the bridge between SQC and engineering specifications.
- ✅ **Six Sigma, TQM, ISO 9001** are all modern extensions of SQC philosophy.
- ✅ **Key buzzwords for full marks:** *assignable cause, chance cause, control limits, process capability, quality consciousness, prevention cost, appraisal cost, failure cost.*

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Quality Management – Philosophies, Models, and Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Total Quality Management – Philosophies, Models, and Implementation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Total Quality Management (TQM) **Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that centers on continuously improving the quality of products, services, and business processes by involving **all members** of the organization — from top management to front-line employees — in a structured, customer-focused framework. It integrates fundamental management techniques, existing improvement efforts, and technical tools under a disciplined approach aimed at long-term success derived from **customer satisfaction** and **societal benefit**. According to the **British Standards Institution (BSI)**, TQM is *"A management philosophy embracing all activities through which the needs of customers, employees, and society are fulfilled through the integrated effort of the whole organization."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TQM is **not** a department; it is an **organization-wide cultural transformation**. Every employee — from the CEO to the sweeper — is responsible for quality. This is what the word *TOTAL* in TQM signifies. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Hospital Operating Theatre Imagine a multi-specialty **hospital**: - The **surgeons** (top management) set the protocols (vision, policy). - The **anaesthetists and nurses** (middle management) execute and monitor. - The **technicians and housekeeping staff** (front-line workers) maintain sterility, instruments, and patient comfort. - The **patient** is the ultimate customer. If any one team fails — say, the sterilization process breaks down — the entire operation fails, regardless of how brilliant the surgeon is. **TQM is exactly this philosophy applied to manufacturing and service industries.** Every link in the chain is responsible for quality, and the success of the organization is judged by the final outcome delivered to the customer. ### 1.3 Core Principles (The Five Pillars of TQM) | S.No | Pillar | Brief Meaning | |------|--------|---------------| | 1 | **Customer Focus** | Quality = Conformance to customer needs (Voice of Customer, VoC) | | 2 | **Total Employee Involvement / Participation** | Every employee owns quality; empowerment via Quality Circles | | 3 | **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** | Incremental, never-ending improvement; PDCA cycle | | 4 | **Process-Centric Approach** | Improve the process, and the output improves automatically | | 5 | **Integrated System** | All departments work as an interconnected supply chain | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric Recognized by KTU Board:** The most universally accepted TQM metric is **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** measured on a **5-point Likert Scale** (1 = Highly Dissatisfied to 5 = Highly Satisfied). A CSI of **≥ 4.5** is considered world-class. ### 1.4 Evolution of Quality Thought (Quick Timeline) | Era | Period | Dominant Philosophy | |-----|--------|---------------------| | Inspection Era | Pre-1900 | 100% inspection, sorting | | Quality Control (QC) | 1900–1940 | Statistical methods (Shewhart) | | Quality Assurance (QA) | 1940–1970 | System-based, documentation | | Strategic Quality Management | 1970–1990 | Cost of quality, strategic planning | | **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | **1980–Present** | **Customer-driven, culture-based, company-wide** | | Six Sigma Era | 1990–Present | Data-driven defect reduction | | Lean Six Sigma | 2000–Present | Waste elimination + defect reduction | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Quality Gurus and Their Philosophies TQM did not emerge spontaneously. It was forged through the life-work of several **"Quality Gurus."** KTU frequently asks direct 3-mark or 7-mark questions comparing these gurus. #### A. Dr. W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993) — The Father of Modern Quality - **Core Philosophy:** Quality is built into the **system**; 94% of failures are due to the system and management, only 6% to workers (the famous **Deming 85/15 Rule**, later refined). - **The Deming Cycle (PDCA):** Plan → Do → Check → Act (also called the **Deming Wheel / Shewhart Cycle**). - **14 Points for Management:** Including "Create constancy of purpose," "Adopt the new philosophy," "Drive out fear," "Institute training on the job," etc. - **Seven Deadly Diseases:** Lack of constancy of purpose, emphasis on short-term profits, evaluation by performance ratings, mobility of management, running a company on visible figures alone, excessive medical costs, excessive costs of warranty. #### B. Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008) — The Customer Champion - **Core Philosophy:** Quality = **Fitness for Use** (defined the first time, replacing the older "conformance to specification"). - **Juran's Quality Trilogy:** 1. **Quality Planning** — Identify customers, determine needs, develop product features. 2. **Quality Control** — Evaluate actual performance vs. goals; act on differences. 3. **Quality Improvement** — Establish infrastructure, identify improvement projects, apply statistical methods. #### C. Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001) — The Zero Defects Proponent - **Core Philosophy:** **"Quality is Free."** The cost of preventing defects is always less than the cost of correcting them. - **Four Absolutes of Quality:** 1. Quality = **Conformance to requirements** (not elegance). 2. Prevention, not inspection. 3. Zero defects performance standard. 4. Measure quality by the **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)**. #### D. Kaoru Ishikawa (1915–1989) — The Father of Quality Circles - **Core Philosophy:** **Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC)** — quality is everyone's responsibility, including top management. - **Tool Contribution:** **Ishikawa Diagram** (Fishbone / Cause-and-Effect Diagram) — for root-cause analysis. - **Quality Circles:** Small groups of 6–12 workers who meet regularly to solve work-related quality problems. #### E. Armand V. Feigenbaum (1922–2014) — Originator of TQC - First to coin the term **"Total Quality Control"** (1956). - Emphasized **"Quality is everyone's job, not just the inspector's."** - Identified **9 M's of Quality**: Markets, Money, Management, Men, Motivation, Materials, Machines, Methods, Mounting requirements. ### 2.2 TQM Models and Frameworks #### A. EFQM Excellence Model (European Foundation for Quality Management) A non-prescriptive framework based on **9 criteria** divided into: - **5 Enablers** (How results are achieved): Leadership, People, Strategy, Partnerships, Processes, Resources. - **4 Results** (What is achieved): People Results, Customer Results, Society Results, Business Results. **RADAR Logic:** Results → Approach → Deploy → Assess → Refine. #### B. Malcolm Baldrige Criteria for Performance Excellence (USA) Seven categories: 1. Leadership 2. Strategic Planning 3. Customer Focus 4. Measurement, Analysis, and Knowledge Management 5. Workforce Focus 6. Operations Focus 7. Results #### C. ISO 9000 Series A set of **international standards** for Quality Management Systems (QMS), currently the global benchmark. **ISO 9001:2015** is the latest version with **7 Quality Management Principles**: 1. Customer Focus 2. Leadership 3. Engagement of People 4. Process Approach 5. Improvement 6. Evidence-based Decision Making 7. Relationship Management ### 2.3 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The Economic Engine of TQM TQM justifies itself financially through the **Cost of Quality** model, comprising **4 cost categories**: | Category | Description | Example | |----------|-------------|---------| | **Prevention Costs (P)** | Costs to prevent defects | Training, quality planning, supplier capability | | **Appraisal Costs (A)** | Costs to detect defects | Inspection, testing, calibration | | **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** | Costs of defects found before delivery | Rework, scrap, re-inspection | | **External Failure Costs (EFC)** | Costs of defects found after delivery | Warranty, returns, legal liability, loss of goodwill | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula | Symbol / Description | Units / Boundary | |---------|----------------------|------------------| | $$\text{Total COQ} = P + A + IFC + EFC$$ | Sum of all four quality costs | Currency (₹, $) | | $$\text{COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality)} = IFC + EFC$$ | Cost due to failures only | Currency | | $$\text{Optimum Quality Level}$$ | Point where **marginal prevention cost = marginal failure cost** | Economics principle | | $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O}$$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | $D$ = defects; $N$ = units; $O$ = opportunities/unit | | $$\text{Yield (\%)} = \frac{N - D}{N} \times 100$$ | First-pass yield | Percentage (0–100) | | $$\text{RTY (Rolled Throughput Yield)} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$ | Probability of zero defects through $n$ stages | Decimal (0–1) | | $$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$ | Process Capability Index | $C_p \geq 1.33$ is capable | | $$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$ | Process Capability accounting for centering | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ | | $$\sigma \text{ Level (Six Sigma)} = 0.8406 + 29.37 \times Y - 1.891 \times 10^{-3} \times Y^2 + 10^{-11} \times Y^3$$ | Long-term sigma from yield $Y$ (Westgard) | $Y$ in % | | $$\text{CSI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$$ | Customer Satisfaction Index (weighted) | 1–5 Likert scale | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Rule:** When using absolute value or limits inside formulas (e.g., $\vert x \vert$ or limits like USL–LSL), always use `\vert` or `\mid` — never the vertical bar `|` — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax in your answer sheets. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility TQM is applied across: - **Manufacturing:** Toyota Production System (Lean + TQM). - **Software:** CMMI Level 5, ISO 9001 certified IT firms. - **Healthcare:** NABH accreditation in Indian hospitals (NEP 2020-aligned skill-based quality in biomedical engineering). - **Aviation & Defense:** AS9100 standard (TQM for aerospace). - **Indian Industry:** Tata Steel (Deming Prize, 2019), Infosys, Maruti Suzuki. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section derives the key analytical relationships examiners love to test in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Quality Cost (Economic Equilibrium of Quality) **Concept:** As we invest more in **prevention**, **appraisal**, and **internal controls**, the **failure costs** fall. There is an optimum point where the **total COQ is minimized**. Let: - $P(x)$ = Prevention cost as a function of prevention investment $x$ (typically linear or convex). - $F(x)$ = Combined Failure Cost = $IFC(x) + EFC(x)$ (a decreasing convex function). - $A(x)$ = Appraisal cost (assumed constant or slowly rising). The total quality cost is: $$C_{\text{total}}(x) = P(x) + A(x) + F(x)$$ To find the **optimum**, take the first derivative and set it to zero: $$\frac{dC_{\text{total}}}{dx} = \frac{dP}{dx} + \frac{dA}{dx} + \frac{dF}{dx} = 0$$ Since $\frac{dF}{dx} < 0$ (failure costs decrease with $x$) and $\frac{dP}{dx} > 0$ (prevention costs increase with $x$), the equilibrium is: $$\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{dF}{dx}$$ **Interpretation:** At the optimum, the **marginal cost of preventing one more defect equals the marginal cost saved by avoiding that defect**. Investing beyond this point actually *increases* total cost. > **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):** > A company has data: $P(x) = 0.5x$, $A(x) = 10$, $F(x) = 100 - 10x + 0.4x^2$, where $x$ is in lakhs of ₹. > > $$C_{\text{total}} = 0.5x + 10 + 100 - 10x + 0.4x^2 = 0.4x^2 - 9.5x + 110$$ > > $$\frac{dC}{dx} = 0.8x - 9.5 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x^* = \frac{9.5}{0.8} = 11.875 \text{ lakhs}$$ > > $$C_{\min} = 0.4(11.875)^2 - 9.5(11.875) + 110 = 56.41 - 112.81 + 110 = 53.60 \text{ lakhs}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Six Sigma Yield to Sigma Level Conversion The relationship between yield and sigma level is empirical (developed by Motorola). For a **1.5 sigma shift** (long-term model), the short-term-to-long-term conversion is: $$Y_{\text{LT}} = Y_{\text{ST}} - 1.5\sigma \text{ (shift)}$$ Using the **standard normal table**, yield values correspond to z-scores: | Yield (%) | Sigma Level | |-----------|-------------| | 99.9997 | 6.0 | | 99.99966 | 6.0 (LT, with 1.5σ shift) | | 99.73 | 3.0 | | 93.32 | 2.0 | | 69.13 | 1.0 | For KTU problems, use the **Westgard formula** (given earlier) for any yield: $$\sigma = 0.8406 + 29.37Y - 1.891 \times 10^{-3} Y^2 + 10^{-11} Y^3$$ where $Y$ is expressed in **percentage** (e.g., 99.0, not 0.99). ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) In a **multi-stage process**, the overall yield is the product of individual stage yields: $$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$ **Derivation logic:** - At stage 1, probability of a defect-free unit = $Y_1$. - At stage 2, conditional probability = $Y_2$. - For independent stages, joint probability = $Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \cdots \times Y_n$. **Example:** A 3-stage process has yields: $Y_1 = 0.95$, $Y_2 = 0.92$, $Y_3 = 0.98$. $$RTY = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 = 0.8565 = 85.65\%$$ So the process has a **14.35% defect rate**, even though no single stage is poor. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Gurus at a Glance (Comparison Table) | Aspect | **Deming** | **Juran** | **Crosby** | **Ishikawa** | **Feigenbaum** | |--------|------------|-----------|------------|--------------|----------------| | Focus | System | Customer | Conformance | People | Cost & Company-wide | | Quality Definition | Predictable uniformity | Fitness for use | Conformance to requirements | Customer satisfaction | Customer-defined | | Tool/Framework | PDCA, 14 Points | Trilogy | 4 Absolutes, Zero Defects | Fishbone, QC Circles | Total Quality Control | | Worker Emphasis | High | Medium | Medium | Very High | High | | Best Known For | 14 Points for Mgmt | Quality Trilogy | "Quality is Free" | Cause-and-Effect | TQC concept | | Origin | USA/Japan | USA | USA | Japan | USA | ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Cost Behavior in TQM Implementation Phases | Phase | Prevention | Appraisal | Internal Failure | External Failure | Total COQ | |-------|------------|-----------|------------------|------------------|-----------| | Phase 1: Reactive (Inspection era) | Low | Low | High | Very High | **Highest** | | Phase 2: Preventive (TQM Intro) | ↑ Increasing | ↑ Increasing | ↓ Decreasing | ↓ Decreasing | Falling | | Phase 3: Optimal | Maximum useful | Stabilized | Low | Low | **Minimum** | | Phase 4: Over-investment | Excessive | Excessive | Very Low | Very Low | Rising again | > **Examiner's Note (KTU):** The most common trap in COQ problems is assuming Prevention and Appraisal are *bad* costs. They are **value-added costs**, while Failure Costs are **non-value-added costs**. TQM aims to convert failure costs into prevention costs. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 TQM Framework — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[CUSTOMER FOCUS
Voice of Customer VoC] --> B[TQM CORE
Leadership Commitment] B --> C[PROCESS MANAGEMENT
PDCA Cycle] B --> D[PEOPLE MANAGEMENT
Quality Circles and Training] B --> E[CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT
Kaizen and Six Sigma] C --> F[MEASUREMENT and ANALYSIS
SPC and SQC Tools] D --> F E --> F F --> G[QUALITY OUTPUT
Conformance and Customer Satisfaction] G --> H{Satisfied Customer?} H -- Yes --> I[LOYALTY, REVENUE, GROWTH] H -- No --> J[RETURNS, WARRANTY, REWORK] J --> A ``` ### 4.2 Deming's PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR P[PLAN
Define problem and analyze process] --> D[DO
Implement on small scale pilot] D --> C[CHECK
Measure results against objectives] C --> A[ACT
Standardize or revise based on learning] A --> P ``` > **Annotation:** The PDCA cycle is **continuous** — there is no end point. Each loop makes the process incrementally better, embodying Kaizen. ### 4.3 Juran's Quality Trilogy Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB QP[QUALITY PLANNING
Identify customers and needs] --> QC[QUALITY CONTROL
Evaluate vs goals and act on gaps] QC --> QI[QUALITY IMPROVEMENT
Establish infrastructure and projects] QI --> QP ``` ### 4.4 TQM Implementation Roadmap (10-Step Logical Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB S1[Step 1: Top Management Commitment] --> S2[Step 2: Customer Focus and VoC] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Quality Policy and Mission Statement] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Identify Critical Processes] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Employee Training and Quality Circles] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Establish Performance Metrics] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Measure Analyze Improve Control] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Supplier Partnership and SQM] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Continuous Improvement Kaizen] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Benchmarking and Recognition] S10 --> S9 ``` ### 4.5 Comparison Architecture: TQM vs Six Sigma vs ISO 9000 ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A[TQM] A1[Philosophy and Culture] A2[Everyone Responsible] A3[Long term Customer Focus] end subgraph B[Six Sigma] B1[Data driven Methodology] B2[3.4 DPMO Target] B3[DMAIC Framework] end subgraph C[ISO 9000] C1[Standards based Certification] C2[Documented QMS] C3[Audit and Compliance] end A --> D[All Integrated in Modern Industry] B --> D C --> D ``` > **Annotation for KTU:** While TQM is a *philosophy*, Six Sigma is a *methodology* and ISO 9000 is a *standard*. They are **complementary**, not competing. Many KTU questions ask for a comparative study — the diagram above is your visual key. ### 4.6 The Cost of Quality Curve (Optimum Visualization) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Curve[Cost Behavior vs Prevention Investment] direction LR P1[Low Prevention] --> P2[Optimal Prevention] --> P3[Over Prevention] end P1 -.->|FC Very High
PC Very Low| R1[High Total COQ] P2 -.->|FC Equals MC of Prevention| R2[Minimum Total COQ] P3 -.->|FC Low
PC Excessive| R3[Rising Total COQ] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1.** Define Total Quality Management. List any four of its key principles. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a holistic, customer-oriented management philosophy that seeks to integrate all organizational functions — marketing, finance, design, production, and service — to continuously improve the quality of goods and services, with the active participation of all employees at every level. > > **Four Key Principles (any four, 0.5 each):** > 1. Customer Focus (internal and external) > 2. Total Employee Involvement and Empowerment > 3. Continuous Improvement (Kaizen) > 4. Process-Centric Decision Making > 5. Integrated System / Strategic Approach > 6. Fact-based Decision Making *(Strictly map each principle to its marks. Use bullet points, not paragraphs.)* --- #### **Q2.** Compare the quality philosophies of Deming and Juran. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks — tabular format preferred):** | Aspect | **W. Edwards Deming** | **Joseph M. Juran** | |--------|----------------------|---------------------| | Definition of Quality | A predictable degree of uniformity and dependability | Fitness for Use | | Key Contribution | 14 Points for Management; PDCA Cycle | Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement) | | Approach | Statistical, System-based | Managerial, Customer-based | | Primary Tool | Statistical Process Control (SPC) | Pareto Analysis, Quality Planning | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *Juran = Quality Trilogy* and *Deming = PDCA* and stop. You will only get **partial marks**. Always elaborate **at least one sentence per row**, especially the *philosophical difference* (Deming = system orientation; Juran = customer orientation). --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks):** **Q3 (a)** Explain the Deming Cycle (PDCA) in detail. How does it support continuous improvement in an engineering organization? `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]` **Q3 (b)** Discuss the Cost of Quality (COQ) model. Illustrate with a numerical example how the optimum quality level is determined. `(7 marks)` #### OR #### **Question B (14 Marks):** **Q4 (a)** Compare and contrast the philosophies of Philip Crosby and Kaoru Ishikawa. Which, in your opinion, is more suitable for the Indian manufacturing sector, and why? `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Q4 (b)** Explain the structure of the EFQM Excellence Model and the Malcolm Baldrige Criteria. How are they used as benchmarking tools for TQM implementation? `(7 marks)` --- ### Complete Model Solutions --- #### **Q3 (a) Solution — Deming Cycle (PDCA)** `(7 marks)` **Introduction (1 mark):** The PDCA cycle, also called the **Deming Wheel** or **Shewhart Cycle**, is the foundational iterative model for continuous improvement, originally conceived by Walter Shewhart and popularized by W. Edwards Deming. **The Four Phases (4 marks — 1 each):** 1. **PLAN:** Identify the problem, analyze the existing process, set objectives, and design a hypothesis for improvement. Tools: Pareto chart, Ishikawa diagram, FMEA. 2. **DO:** Execute the plan on a small scale (pilot run) to test the hypothesis. Collect data rigorously. 3. **CHECK (or STUDY):** Compare the actual results against the expected results using statistical tools (control charts, hypothesis testing). Identify gaps. 4. **ACT:** If successful, standardize the change across the organization (update SOPs). If not, refine the plan and repeat. Lessons are documented. **Application in Engineering (2 marks):** - **Manufacturing:** Reduce defect rate in CNC machining by adjusting spindle speed (PDCA loop). - **Software:** Iterative Agile sprints follow PDCA implicitly. - **Continuous Improvement:** The cycle never ends — after Act, a new Plan begins at a higher baseline, embodying **Kaizen**. **Diagram (recommended):** Draw the circular PDCA loop with arrows. *(0 marks if missing in KTU exam — always include diagram.)* --- #### **Q3 (b) Solution — Cost of Quality (COQ) Model** `(7 marks)` **Definition (1 mark):** COQ is the total cost incurred to ensure product quality and the costs arising from failure to meet quality. It comprises **Prevention (P)**, **Appraisal (A)**, **Internal Failure (IFC)**, and **External Failure (EFC)** costs. **Component Breakdown (2 marks):** | Cost Type | Nature | Examples | |-----------|--------|----------| | Prevention | Value-added | Training, Quality planning, Supplier audits | | Appraisal | Value-added | Incoming inspection, In-process testing | | Internal Failure | Non-value-added | Scrap, Rework, Re-inspection | | External Failure | Non-value-added | Warranty, Returns, Litigation, Lost goodwill | **Numerical Example (3 marks):** A firm produces 10,000 units/month. Annual costs: - $P = \text{₹}2{,}00{,}000$ - $A = \text{₹}1{,}50{,}000$ - $IFC = \text{₹}4{,}00{,}000$ - $EFC = \text{₹}6{,}00{,}000$ $$\text{Total COQ} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}13{,}50{,}000$$ **COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality):** $$COPQ = IFC + EFC = 4{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹}10{,}00{,}000$$ This means **74%** of the quality spend is wasted on failures. TQM aims to shift this balance. **Optimum Determination (1 mark):** The optimum is where marginal prevention cost equals marginal reduction in failure cost — the curve of total COQ reaches its **minimum**. --- #### **Q4 (a) Solution — Crosby vs Ishikawa** `(7 marks)` **Crosby's Philosophy (2 marks):** - Defined quality as **"conformance to requirements"** (not elegance). - Famous for the maxim **"Quality is Free"** — arguing prevention always pays. - Propagated the **Zero Defects** standard (not "acceptable quality level"). - **Four Absolutes:** Conformance, Prevention (not inspection), Zero Defects performance standard, Measure by Price of Non-Conformance (PONC). - **Cost-of-Quality framework** — categorized quality costs into P, A, F (Failure = IFC + EFC). **Ishikawa's Philosophy (2 marks):** - Coined **"Company-Wide Quality Control (CWQC)"** — quality is everyone's responsibility. - Created the **Fishbone (Cause-and-Effect) Diagram** for root-cause analysis (the "6 Ms": Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature). - Pioneered **Quality Circles** (small worker groups, typically 6–12 members, meeting weekly to solve quality problems). - Emphasized **inter-departmental cooperation** and **data-based analysis**. **Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Aspect | **Crosby** | **Ishikawa** | |--------|-----------|--------------| | Quality Definition | Conformance to requirements | Customer satisfaction | | Key Slogan | Quality is Free | Quality is everyone's job | | Tool | Zero Defects Program | Fishbone, Quality Circles | | Approach | Top-down cultural change | Bottom-up employee empowerment | | Focus | Cost avoidance | Statistical analysis and teamwork | **Suitability for India (1 mark):** For the **Indian manufacturing sector** (with abundant semi-skilled labour and varied process maturity), **Ishikawa's approach is more suitable**. Reason: Quality Circles empower floor-level workers, develop skills, and align with India's demographic dividend. Crosby's Zero Defects, while aspirational, requires a more mature documentation culture. --- #### **Q4 (b) Solution — EFQM and Baldrige Models** `(7 marks)` **EFQM Excellence Model (3 marks):** - Founded by the **European Foundation for Quality Management (EFQM)** in 1988. - Based on the premise that *"Excellent results with respect to Performance, Customers, People and Society are achieved through Leadership driving Strategy and Policy, delivered through People, Partnerships and Resources, and Processes."* - **9 Criteria:** - **5 Enablers:** Leadership, People, Strategy, Partnerships & Resources, Processes. - **4 Results:** People Results, Customer Results, Society Results, Business Results. - **RADAR Logic:** Results → Approach → Deploy → Assess → Refine. **Malcolm Baldrige Criteria (3 marks):** - Established in 1987 by the **U.S. Congress** in honor of Malcolm Baldrige, former Secretary of Commerce. - **7 Categories:** 1. Leadership (120 pts) 2. Strategic Planning (85 pts) 3. Customer Focus (85 pts) 4. Measurement, Analysis, and Knowledge Management (90 pts) 5. Workforce Focus (85 pts) 6. Operations Focus (85 pts) 7. Results (450 pts) - Used in the **U.S. Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (MBNQA)**. **Comparison as Benchmarking Tools (1 mark):** Both models are **non-prescriptive frameworks** — they tell organizations *what* to achieve, not *how*. They enable self-assessment against world-class benchmarks. Indian organizations like **Tata Steel, Maruti Suzuki, and Infosys** use these as diagnostic tools to identify improvement areas. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Common Deductions:** > 1. ❌ Confusing *EFQM with ISO* — EFQM is a *model*, ISO 9001 is a *certifiable standard*. > 2. ❌ Forgetting to write the full form of abbreviations at least once (RADAR, CWQC, PONC). > 3. ❌ Drawing EFQM as a *pyramid* — it is officially a **nine-box diagram** with two horizontal bars (Enablers and Results). > 4. ❌ In numerical problems, not writing the units (₹, %, units). > 5. ❌ In Crosby questions, confusing *Prevention cost* with *Quality cost of prevention as wasteful* — they are *value-added*. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **TQM = Total Quality Management** — organization-wide philosophy, customer-centric, continuous improvement. - [ ] **Five Pillars:** Customer Focus, Total Employee Involvement, Continuous Improvement, Process Approach, Integrated System. - [ ] **Deming:** System focus; 14 Points; **PDCA** cycle; 85/15 rule (or 94/6). - [ ] **Juran:** **Fitness for Use**; Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement); Pareto-based. - [ ] **Crosby:** **Conformance to requirements**; **Zero Defects**; **Quality is Free**; 4 Absolutes; PONC metric. - [ ] **Ishikawa:** **Fishbone diagram** (6 Ms); **Quality Circles**; **CWQC**. - [ ] **Feigenbaum:** Coined **TQC** (1956); 9 M's of Quality. - [ ] **Models:** **EFQM** (5 Enablers + 4 Results; RADAR); **Baldrige** (7 categories); **ISO 9000** (7 QMS Principles, latest version 9001:2015). - [ ] **COQ Formula:** $COQ = P + A + IFC + EFC$; Optimum at marginal cost equality. - [ ] **Six Sigma Formula:** $DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O}$; Long-term target = 3.4 DPMO. - [ ] **RTY:** $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ — joint probability of zero defects through n stages. - [ ] **Process Capability:** $C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$; $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$. - [ ] **PDCA** never ends — represents **Kaizen**. - [ ] **Always include a diagram** in 7-mark and 14-mark answers — KTU examiners reward visualization. - [ ] **Cite real Indian examples** (Tata Steel, Maruti, Infosys) for contextual credit in 14-mark essays. - [ ] **Avoid markdown special characters** in answer sheets — use plain $\vert$ in place of $\mid$ if writing in LaTeX, never the bare pipe `|` in tables. --- > **End of Module 1 Notes — Topic: Total Quality Management – Philosophies, Models, and Implementation** > **Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Operating Characteristic Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve** of an acceptance sampling plan is a graphical representation that depicts the **probability of accepting a lot ($P_a$)** as a function of the incoming lot quality, expressed either as the **fraction defective ($p$)**, **defects per hundred units**, or the **defects per unit ($\lambda$)**, when the lot is subjected to a specific sampling inspection plan characterized by the triplet $(N, n, c)$.

Mathematically, the OC curve is the locus of all points:

$$P_a = f(p)$$

where $p \in [0, 1]$ for attribute plans, and the function $f$ is determined by the lot size $N$, sample size $n$, and acceptance number $c$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):** The OC curve is the single most important descriptor of a sampling plan. It simultaneously encodes the discriminating power of the plan, the producer's risk ($\alpha$), and the consumer's risk ($\beta$). Mastery of OC curve construction is essential for KTU ESE and continuous assessment evaluations.

### 1.2 Notational Conventions in KTU 2024

| Symbol | Standard KTU Meaning |
|:------:|:---------------------|
| $N$ | Lot size (number of items in the submitted lot) |
| $n$ | Sample size drawn for inspection |
| $c$ | Acceptance number (maximum defectives allowed in sample) |
| $p$ | True incoming fraction defective (process quality level) |
| $P_a$ | Probability of acceptance (ordinate of OC curve) |
| $\alpha$ | Producer's Risk (Type I error probability) |
| $\beta$ | Consumer's Risk (Type II error probability) |
| AQL | Acceptable Quality Level |
| LTPD | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective |
| $\lambda$ | Average number of defects per unit |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Quality Inspector's Dilemma"

Imagine you are a customs officer at a border checkpoint. Every truck arriving carries **100 sealed boxes** ($N = 100$), and you cannot open all of them. You randomly inspect **$n = 10$ boxes** and allow the truck to pass if **at most $c = 1$ box** is found non-compliant.

- If the truck is genuinely **clean** (low $p$), almost every time your small sample will be free of defects, so $P_a \approx 1$ — the curve starts near the top-left.
- If the truck is **highly contaminated** (high $p$), your sample will almost always catch a defect, so $P_a \approx 0$ — the curve falls to the bottom-right.
- The middle region is the "**gray zone**" where the inspector's decision is genuinely uncertain.

The OC curve traces this **transition from acceptance to rejection** as the underlying lot quality deteriorates. It is essentially the **"fingerprint"** of any acceptance sampling plan.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ideal OC Curve vs. Practical OC Curve:** An *ideal* OC curve would be a vertical step-function jumping from $P_a = 1$ to $P_a = 0$ at a single critical $p$-value. In reality, sampling is stochastic, so the OC curve is always a smooth, S-shaped (sigmoidal) decreasing function. The *steeper* the curve, the *better* the discriminating power of the plan.

### 1.4 The Two Pillars: Producer's Risk and Consumer's Risk

Two critical points on the OC curve define the contractual agreement between buyer and supplier:

$$\text{Producer's Risk: } P_a(p_1 = \text{AQL}) = 1 - \alpha \quad \text{(typically } \alpha = 0.05\text{)}$$

$$\text{Consumer's Risk: } P_a(p_2 = \text{LTPD}) = \beta \quad \text{(typically } \beta = 0.10\text{)}$$

These two points, together with the curve itself, define the **acceptance region** and the **rejection region** of the statistical contract.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown — How an OC Curve is Constructed

The construction of an OC curve proceeds through the following rigorous sequence:

1. **Define the sampling plan parameters:** Specify $N$, $n$, and $c$.
2. **Select the underlying probability model:** Choose between the hypergeometric (Type A), binomial (Type B), or Poisson (Type C) distributions based on the ratio $n/N$.
3. **Compute $P_a$** at multiple values of $p$ ranging from $0$ to $1$.
4. **Plot $P_a$ (y-axis) against $p$ (x-axis)** and connect the points with a smooth curve.
5. **Mark the AQL and LTPD reference points** to visualize producer's and consumer's risks.

### 2.2 The Three Types of OC Curves (KTU Mandatory Distinction)

#### Type A OC Curve — Hypergeometric Distribution (Finite Population Correction)

Used when the sampling fraction $n/N \geq 0.10$. Treats the lot as a finite population without replacement.

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{\binom{Np}{i} \binom{N - Np}{n - i}}{\binom{N}{n}}$$

Here $Np$ represents the exact number of defectives in the lot. This is the *exact* but computationally intensive form.

#### Type B OC Curve — Binomial Distribution (Infinite Lot Assumption)

Used when $n/N \leq 0.10$. Assumes the lot is large enough that successive draws are statistically independent.

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1 - p)^{n - i}$$

This is the **workhorse formula** used in most KTU problems and is the standard for plan design.

#### Type C OC Curve — Poisson Approximation (Rare Defects)

Used when $p \leq 0.10$ and $n \geq 30$. The Poisson limit theorem justifies:

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np} (np)^{i}}{i!} = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{i}}{i!}$$

where $\lambda = np$ is the expected number of defectives in the sample.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Heuristic:** When $np \leq 5$, the Poisson approximation is highly accurate and reduces computational load dramatically. When $p > 0.10$ or $n < 30$, use the binomial directly.

### 2.3 Reading the OC Curve — Key Operational Decisions

The OC curve allows us to make four critical engineering decisions:

- **Discriminating Power:** A steep curve between AQL and LTPD indicates a high-quality plan.
- **Producer's Protection:** At $p = $ AQL, $P_a$ should be high (e.g., $\geq 0.95$).
- **Consumer's Protection:** At $p = $ LTPD, $P_a$ should be low (e.g., $\leq 0.10$).
- **Indifference Quality Level ($p_0$):** The $p$ value where $P_a = 0.50$, representing the boundary of "equal probability of acceptance or rejection."

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula Description | Mathematical Expression | Domain of Validity |
|:--------------------|:------------------------|:-------------------|
| Type A (Hypergeometric) Acceptance Probability | $P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \dfrac{\binom{Np}{i}\binom{N - Np}{n - i}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | $n/N \geq 0.10$ |
| Type B (Binomial) Acceptance Probability | $P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{i}(1-p)^{n-i}$ | $n/N \leq 0.10$ |
| Type C (Poisson) Acceptance Probability | $P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^{i}}{i!}$ | $p \leq 0.10$, $n \geq 30$ |
| Producer's Risk Definition | $1 - \alpha = P_a(\text{AQL})$ | $\alpha \approx 0.05$ |
| Consumer's Risk Definition | $\beta = P_a(\text{LTPD})$ | $\beta \approx 0.10$ |
| Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) | $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N}$ | All plans |
| Average Total Inspection (ATI) | $\text{ATI} = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$ | All plans |
| Binomial Coefficient | $\binom{n}{i} = \dfrac{n!}{i!(n-i)!}$ | All integer $n, i$ |
| Average Sample Number (Double) | $\text{ASN} = n_1 + n_2(1 - P_1)$ | Double sampling |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The OC curve is the foundational analytical tool in:

- **Pharmaceutical Quality Assurance:** FDA-mandated acceptance sampling for drug batches.
- **Automotive Industry:** Incoming part inspection from tier-2 suppliers.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** MIL-STD-105E / ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 sampling tables.
- **Aerospace:** Compliance verification per AS9100 standards.
- **Food Safety:** Microbiological contamination lot release decisions.

In every case, the OC curve provides a **statistically defensible, audit-ready** rationale for the accept/reject decision that transcends subjective human judgment.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Hypergeometric to Binomial (Type A → Type B Limit)

We start from the hypergeometric probability of observing exactly $i$ defectives in a sample of size $n$ drawn from a lot of size $N$ containing $Np$ defectives:

$$P(X = i) = \frac{\binom{Np}{i}\binom{N - Np}{n - i}}{\binom{N}{n}}$$

We now expand $\binom{Np}{i}$, $\binom{N - Np}{n-i}$, and $\binom{N}{n}$ using factorials:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X = i) &= \frac{(Np)!}{i!\,(Np - i)!} \cdot \frac{(N - Np)!}{(n - i)!\,(N - Np - n + i)!} \cdot \frac{n!\,(N - n)!}{N!} \\
&= \frac{n!}{i!\,(n - i)!} \cdot \frac{(Np)(Np-1)\cdots(Np-i+1)}{N(N-1)\cdots(N-i+1)} \cdot \frac{(N - Np)(N - Np - 1)\cdots(N - Np - n + i + 1)}{(N - i)(N - i - 1)\cdots(N - n + 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**

- The first factor simplifies directly to $\binom{n}{i}$.
- The second factor is the product of $i$ terms of the form $(Np - k)/N$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, i - 1$.
- As $N \to \infty$ with $p$ fixed, each term $(Np - k)/N \to p$.
- Similarly, the third factor collapses to $(1-p)^{n-i}$ as $N \to \infty$.

Therefore, in the limit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} P(X = i) &= \binom{n}{i} \cdot p^{i} \cdot (1 - p)^{n - i}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is precisely the **binomial probability mass function**, validating the Type B OC curve as the large-$N$ limit of the Type A curve.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: From Binomial to Poisson (Type B → Type C Limit)

We begin with the binomial expression:

$$P(X = i) = \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1 - p)^{n - i}$$

We hold $\lambda = np$ constant while letting $n \to \infty$ and $p \to 0$ such that $np = \lambda$. Substitute $p = \lambda / n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X = i) &= \frac{n!}{i!\,(n-i)!} \left(\frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{i} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n - i} \\
&= \frac{n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-i+1)}{i!} \cdot \frac{\lambda^{i}}{n^{i}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n - i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now we analyze each piece as $n \to \infty$:

- The numerator $n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-i+1)$ divided by $n^{i}$ tends to $1$ (since $i$ is fixed and finite).
- The term $(1 - \lambda/n)^{n-i}$ can be split: $(1 - \lambda/n)^{n} \to e^{-\lambda}$ and $(1 - \lambda/n)^{-i} \to 1$.

Combining these limits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} P(X = i) &= \frac{1}{i!} \cdot \lambda^{i} \cdot e^{-\lambda} \cdot 1 = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{i}}{i!}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Poisson distribution**, justifying the Type C OC curve in the rare-defect regime.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1: Computing an OC Curve Point (Binomial)

**Problem:** For the plan $(n = 20, c = 2)$, calculate $P_a$ at $p = 0.10$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(p = 0.10) &= \sum_{i=0}^{2} \binom{20}{i} (0.10)^{i} (0.90)^{20-i} \\
&= \binom{20}{0}(0.10)^{0}(0.90)^{20} + \binom{20}{1}(0.10)^{1}(0.90)^{19} + \binom{20}{2}(0.10)^{2}(0.90)^{18}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing each term numerically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_0 &= 1 \times 1 \times (0.90)^{20} = 0.1216 \\
\text{Term}_1 &= 20 \times 0.10 \times (0.90)^{19} = 20 \times 0.10 \times 0.1351 = 0.2702 \\
\text{Term}_2 &= 190 \times 0.01 \times (0.90)^{18} = 190 \times 0.01 \times 0.1501 = 0.2852
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(0.10) &= 0.1216 + 0.2702 + 0.2852 = 0.6770
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When the incoming quality is 10% defective, this plan will accept the lot approximately **67.7% of the time** — indicating only moderate protection for the consumer at this quality level.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2: Producer's Risk Calculation

**Problem:** For the plan $(n = 50, c = 2)$ with AQL = 0.02, calculate the producer's risk $\alpha$.

**Solution:** We need $P_a(\text{AQL}) = 1 - \alpha$, so compute $P_a(0.02)$ using the binomial:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(0.02) &= \sum_{i=0}^{2} \binom{50}{i} (0.02)^{i} (0.98)^{50-i} \\
&= \binom{50}{0}(0.02)^{0}(0.98)^{50} + \binom{50}{1}(0.02)^{1}(0.98)^{49} + \binom{50}{2}(0.02)^{2}(0.98)^{48}
\end{aligned}
$$

Numerical evaluation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term}_0 &= 1 \times 1 \times (0.98)^{50} = 0.3642 \\
\text{Term}_1 &= 50 \times 0.02 \times (0.98)^{49} = 50 \times 0.02 \times 0.3716 = 0.3716 \\
\text{Term}_2 &= 1225 \times 0.0004 \times (0.98)^{48} = 1225 \times 0.0004 \times 0.3792 = 0.1858
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(0.02) &= 0.3642 + 0.3716 + 0.1858 = 0.9216
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the producer's risk is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= 1 - P_a(0.02) = 1 - 0.9216 = 0.0784
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** About **7.84% of lots** at AQL quality will be rejected — this is the producer's "punishment" for setting too aggressive a contract.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3: Consumer's Risk Calculation (Poisson Approximation)

**Problem:** For the plan $(n = 30, c = 1)$ with LTPD = 0.10, calculate the consumer's risk $\beta$ using Poisson approximation.

**Solution:** With $\lambda = np = 30 \times 0.10 = 3.0$ and $c = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(0.10) &= \sum_{i=0}^{1} \frac{e^{-3}(3)^{i}}{i!} \\
&= \frac{e^{-3}(3)^{0}}{0!} + \frac{e^{-3}(3)^{1}}{1!} \\
&= e^{-3}(1 + 3) \\
&= 4 e^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Numerical computation: $e^{-3} \approx 0.04979$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(0.10) &= 4 \times 0.04979 = 0.1991
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta = P_a(\text{LTPD}) &= 0.1991 \approx 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** There is a **20% chance** that a lot with 10% defectives will be erroneously accepted. The consumer should demand a stricter plan (e.g., reduce $c$) if $\beta = 0.10$ is required.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example 4: Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

**Problem:** For the plan $(N = 1000, n = 50, c = 1)$ with incoming quality $p = 0.05$, compute AOQ assuming all rejected lots are 100% inspected and defectives replaced.

**Solution:**

First, compute $P_a(0.05)$ using the binomial:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(0.05) &= \sum_{i=0}^{1} \binom{50}{i} (0.05)^{i} (0.95)^{50-i} \\
&= (0.95)^{50} + 50 \times 0.05 \times (0.95)^{49} \\
&= 0.0769 + 50 \times 0.05 \times 0.0810 \\
&= 0.0769 + 0.2025 = 0.2794
\end{aligned}
$$

The fraction of items passing through the system (assumed defect-free after rectification) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AOQ} &= \frac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n)}{N} \\
&= \frac{0.2794 \times 0.05 \times (1000 - 50)}{1000} \\
&= \frac{0.2794 \times 0.05 \times 950}{1000} \\
&= \frac{13.2715}{1000} = 0.01327
\end{aligned}
$$

Expressed as percent: $\text{AOQ} \approx 1.327\%$.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Model

| Condition | Recommended OC Type | Justification |
|:----------|:-------------------|:--------------|
| $n/N \geq 0.10$ | Type A (Hypergeometric) | Finite population correction is non-negligible |
| $n/N \leq 0.10$, $p \leq 0.5$ | Type B (Binomial) | Large-lot approximation is valid |
| $n \geq 30$, $p \leq 0.10$, $np \leq 5$ | Type C (Poisson) | Sparse defect regime, easiest computation |
| $np > 5$ and $p > 0.10$ | Type B (Binomial) | Poisson approximation becomes inaccurate |
| Defects per unit (continuous) | Type C with $\lambda = D \cdot n$ | Used for non-conformities |

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Operating Characteristic (OC) curve. What information does it convey about an acceptance sampling plan?**

**Model Answer:**

The Operating Characteristic (OC) curve is a graph that displays the probability of accepting a lot ($P_a$) as a function of the incoming lot quality, expressed as the fraction defective $p$ for attribute plans. It conveys three critical pieces of information: (i) the **discriminating ability** of the sampling plan between good and bad lots, (ii) the **producer's risk $\alpha$** at the Acceptable Quality Level (AQL), and (iii) the **consumer's risk $\beta$** at the Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD). A steeply descending S-shaped OC curve indicates a highly discriminating plan, whereas a gradually sloping curve suggests poor discrimination. *(3 marks)*

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between Type A, Type B, and Type C OC curves. When is each one used?**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Distribution | When Used | Formula Basis |
|:----:|:-------------|:----------|:--------------|
| **Type A** | Hypergeometric | Finite lot with $n/N \geq 0.10$ | Exact finite-population |
| **Type B** | Binomial | Large lot with $n/N \leq 0.10$ | Sampling with replacement |
| **Type C** | Poisson | Rare defects with $p \leq 0.10$, $n \geq 30$ | Limiting case of binomial |

The Type A curve is exact but computationally expensive; Type B is the standard workhorse; Type C is the simplest approximation for sparse-defect regimes. *(3 marks)*

For $c = 2$, you must sum from $i = 0$ to $i = 2$ (three terms), **not** from $i = 0$ to $i = c - 1$. Off-by-one errors in summation limits cost **up to 2 marks** per problem. #### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Construct the OC curve for the sampling plan $n = 25$, $c = 1$ using the binomial distribution for the following values of $p$: $0.01$, $0.03$, $0.05$, $0.07$, $0.10$, $0.15$. Tabulate your results.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the above plan, find the producer's risk if AQL = 0.03, and the consumer's risk if LTPD = 0.10. Comment on the suitability of this plan.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — OC Curve Tabulation:**

For $c = 1$, the binomial acceptance probability is:

$$P_a(p) = \binom{25}{0}(1-p)^{25} + \binom{25}{1} p (1-p)^{24}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= (1-p)^{25} + 25 p (1-p)^{24}
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluating at each $p$ value:

| $p$ | $(1-p)^{25}$ | $25 p (1-p)^{24}$ | $P_a(p)$ |
|:---:|:-------------:|:-----------------:|:--------:|
| 0.01 | 0.7778 | 0.1964 | 0.9742 |
| 0.03 | 0.4670 | 0.3613 | 0.8283 |
| 0.05 | 0.2774 | 0.3650 | 0.6424 |
| 0.07 | 0.1612 | 0.3034 | 0.4646 |
| 0.10 | 0.0718 | 0.1994 | 0.2712 |
| 0.15 | 0.0172 | 0.0760 | 0.0932 |

The OC curve descends smoothly from $P_a \approx 0.97$ at $p = 0.01$ to $P_a \approx 0.09$ at $p = 0.15$.

**Part (b) — Risk Computation and Interpretation:**

From the tabulation:

- At AQL $= 0.03$: $P_a = 0.8283$, so $\alpha = 1 - 0.8283 = 0.1717$.
- At LTPD $= 0.10$: $P_a = 0.2712$, so $\beta = 0.2712$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= 17.17\% \\
\beta &= 27.12\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Suitability:** Both risks are *unacceptably high* compared to standard contractual thresholds ($\alpha \leq 0.05$, $\beta \leq 0.10$). This plan offers insufficient protection to **both** producer and consumer. To improve, either reduce $c$ to $0$ (single-defect strict plan) or increase $n$. The current plan is suitable only as a *screening* stage in a double-sampling scheme, not as a stand-alone contract.

#### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Poisson approximation to the binomial acceptance probability formula starting from the binomial distribution. State the conditions under which the approximation is valid.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the Poisson approximation, construct the OC curve for the plan $n = 50$, $c = 2$ at the quality levels $p = 0.01$, $0.02$, $0.04$, $0.06$, $0.08$, $0.10$. Identify the AQL (where $P_a \geq 0.95$) and LTPD (where $P_a \leq 0.10$).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Starting from the binomial acceptance probability:

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}$$

Let $\lambda = np$ be held constant as $n \to \infty$, $p \to 0$. Substituting $p = \lambda/n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &= \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{n!}{i!(n-i)!} \left(\frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{i} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-i}
\end{aligned}
$$

As $n \to \infty$, $\frac{n(n-1)\cdots(n-i+1)}{n^{i}} \to 1$ and $\left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n} \to e^{-\lambda}$, giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a &\approx \sum_{i=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{i}}{i!}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conditions of Validity:** (i) $p \leq 0.10$ (rare events), (ii) $n \geq 30$ (large sample), and (iii) $\lambda = np \leq 5$ (sparse mean).

**Part (b) — OC Curve via Poisson:**

For $c = 2$, $n = 50$, the formula is:

$$P_a(p) = e^{-50p} \left[1 + 50p + \frac{(50p)^{2}}{2}\right]$$

Computing for each $p$:

| $p$ | $\lambda = 50p$ | $e^{-\lambda}$ | $1 + \lambda + \lambda^{2}/2$ | $P_a(p)$ |
|:---:|:---------------:|:--------------:|:----------------------------:|:--------:|
| 0.01 | 0.5 | 0.6065 | 1 + 0.5 + 0.125 = 1.625 | 0.9856 |
| 0.02 | 1.0 | 0.3679 | 1 + 1.0 + 0.500 = 2.500 | 0.9197 |
| 0.04 | 2.0 | 0.1353 | 1 + 2.0 + 2.000 = 5.000 | 0.6767 |
| 0.06 | 3.0 | 0.0498 | 1 + 3.0 + 4.500 = 8.500 | 0.4232 |
| 0.08 | 4.0 | 0.0183 | 1 + 4.0 + 8.000 = 13.000 | 0.2381 |
| 0.10 | 5.0 | 0.0067 | 1 + 5.0 + 12.500 = 18.500 | 0.1247 |

**Identification of AQL and LTPD:**

- **AQL:** We seek $p$ such that $P_a \geq 0.95$. From the table, $p = 0.01$ gives $P_a = 0.9856$. Hence AQL $\approx 0.01$ (more precisely, by interpolation, AQL $\approx 0.0125$).
- **LTPD:** We seek $p$ such that $P_a \leq 0.10$. From the table, $p = 0.10$ gives $P_a = 0.1247 > 0.10$. We need to extrapolate; at $p \approx 0.105$, $P_a$ falls below 0.10. Hence LTPD $\approx 0.105$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AQL} &\approx 0.01 \\
\text{LTPD} &\approx 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note:** When the KTU question specifies "find the AQL/LTPD," students often confuse which side of the curve they are referencing. AQL is always on the **left** (low $p$, high $P_a$) side, and LTPD is on the **right** (high $p$, low $P_a$) side. Reversing these yields an inverted conclusion and will lose **all 7 marks** for part (b).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Operating Characteristic Curve (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** OC curve = graph of $P_a$ versus $p$ for a fixed sampling plan $(N, n, c)$.
- **Shape:** Sigmoidal (S-shaped) decreasing curve; steeper is better discrimination.
- **Ideal OC:** Vertical step-function at the indifference quality $p_0$.
- **Type A:** Hypergeometric, used when $n/N \geq 0.10$ (finite lot, exact).
- **Type B:** Binomial, used when $n/N \leq 0.10$ (large lot, workhorse).
- **Type C:** Poisson, used when $p \leq 0.10$ and $n \geq 30$ with $np \leq 5$.
- **Producer's Risk $\alpha$:** Probability of rejecting a good lot at AQL; standard $\alpha = 0.05$.
- **Consumer's Risk $\beta$:** Probability of accepting a bad lot at LTPD; standard $\beta = 0.10$.
- **Indifference Quality $p_0$:** Quality level at which $P_a = 0.50$.
- **Binomial Formula:** $P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \binom{n}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{n-i}$.
- **Poisson Formula:** $P_a = \sum_{i=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^{i}}{i!}$.
- **AOQ Formula:** $\text{AOQ} = P_a \cdot p \cdot (N - n) / N$.
- **ATI Formula:** $\text{ATI} = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$.
- **Plan Improvement:** Decrease $c$ or increase $n$ to steepen the OC curve.
- **KTU Pitfall #1:** Off-by-one in summation limits (always explicitly write $\sum_{i=0}^{c}$).
- **KTU Pitfall #2:** Confusing AQL (left) with LTPD (right) on the curve.
- **KTU Pitfall #3:** Using Poisson when $p > 0.10$ — accuracy degrades sharply.
- **Real-World Link:** MIL-STD-105E / ANSI Z1.4 sampling tables are pre-computed OC curves.
- **Companion Curves:** Always remember OC, AOQ, ATI, and ASN are interrelated.
- **Final Verification:** When $p = 0$, $P_a = 1$ (always accept); when $p = 1$, $P_a = 0$ (always reject).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
