They design the curriculum, train Black Belts and Green Belts, audit project statistical rigour, and lead the most complex, cross-functional projects. MBBs are *Consulted* throughout DMAIC as the resident experts on advanced tools (regression, DOE, hypothesis testing). **(1.5 marks)** 4. **Black Belts (BB):** Full-time project leaders who apply DMAIC rigorously. They own the project plan, conduct Measure/Analyze/Improve phases, mentor Green Belts, and deliver quantified savings (e.g., DPMO reduction). BBs are *Responsible* for project delivery from Measure through Improve. **(1.5 marks)** 5. **Green Belts (GB):** Subject-matter experts who participate in projects part-time. They lead sub-projects, collect data, run smaller DMAIC cycles, and act as the bridge between BBs and Yellow Belts. **(1 mark)** 6. **Yellow Belts (YB):** Frontline employees with foundational Six Sigma awareness. They participate in brainstorming (Define), support data collection (Measure), and sustain improvements at the gemba (Control). **(1 mark)** **(b)** *Construct the RACI matrix for the following Six Sigma project activities in a CNC machining cell:* 1. *Approve ₹20 lakh project budget.* 2. *Conduct Gauge R&R study.* 3. *Run a 2k full factorial DOE on cutting speed and feed.* 4. *Implement Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts on the shop floor.* 5. *Sustain the improved process for 12 months post-handover.* *Clearly mark who is Responsible (R), Accountable (A), Consulted (C), and Informed (I).* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | # | Activity | R (Responsible) | A (Accountable) | C (Consulted) | I (Informed) | |---|----------|-----------------|-----------------|---------------|--------------| | 1 | Approve ₹20 L budget | Finance Mgr | Champion | Exec. Leadership | Black Belt, Process Owner | | 2 | Gauge R&R study | Black Belt | Champion | Master Black Belt | Green Belt, Process Owner | | 3 | 2k factorial DOE | Black Belt | Champion | Master Black Belt | Green Belt | | 4 | SPC charts on shop floor | Green Belt | Process Owner | Black Belt | Yellow Belt, Champion | | 5 | Sustain process 12 months | Process Owner | Process Owner | Black Belt (audit) | Champion, Exec. Leadership | **Justification (each row carries ~1.2 marks):** - *Row 1*: Budget approval is a strategic decision — only the Champion is Accountable. **(1.2 marks)** - *Row 2*: Gauge R&R is a Measure-phase statistical study, owned by the Black Belt with Master Black Belt as methodology consultant. **(1.2 marks)** - *Row 3*: DOE is an Improve-phase advanced tool — Black Belt executes; MBB validates design. **(1.2 marks)** - *Row 4*: SPC is a Control-phase activity handed over to Green Belts and Process Owners; Black Belt consults during the initial setup. **(1.2 marks)** - *Row 5*: Post-handover sustainability is the *Process Owner's* charter — they are both Accountable and Responsible; Black Belt audits periodically. **(1.2 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 3]** **(a)** *Explain the responsibilities of the Champion, Black Belt, Green Belt, and Process Owner across each phase of DMAIC. Use a phase-wise table.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | DMAIC Phase | Champion | Black Belt | Green Belt | Process Owner | |-------------|----------|------------|------------|---------------| | **Define** | Selects project, signs charter, allocates team | Drafts project charter, defines CTQ | Provides subject-matter input on VOC | Identifies the process and its boundaries | | **Measure** | Approves data-collection plan | Designs MSA, baseline sigma | Collects data, runs capability studies | Provides access to process data | | **Analyze** | Reviews interim findings | Runs regression, hypothesis tests, root-cause analysis | Validates root causes on shop floor | Confirms analysis against process reality | | **Improve** | Approves pilot results | Designs and runs DOE, pilots solutions | Implements pilot solutions | Approves operational changes | | **Control** | Sign-off for project closure | Develops control plan, training materials | Updates SOPs, trains Yellow Belts | Owns controls, monitors KPIs, sustains gains | (Each row ≈ 1.4 marks; explicit linkage to roles and DMAIC is mandatory.) **(b)** *In a printed-circuit-board (PCB) assembly line, 850 defects are observed in 4,250 assembled boards. Each board has 6 soldering opportunities. Compute DPMO, DPO, and the process sigma level. Based on these metrics, justify which Six Sigma roles should be deployed and what their responsibilities would be.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute DPO.** $$N \times O = 4250 \times 6 = 25{,}500 \text{ opportunities}$$ $$\text{DPO} = \dfrac{D}{N \times O} = \dfrac{850}{25{,}500} = 0.03333$$ **Step 2 — Compute DPMO.** $$\text{DPMO} = 0.03333 \times 10^{6} = 33{,}333 \text{ defects per million opportunities}$$ **Step 3 — Compute Process Sigma.** $$\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{33{,}333}{10^{6}}\right) + 0.5\right) + 1.5$$ $$\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(0.5 \times 0.96667 + 0.5\right) + 1.5$$ $$\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(0.98333\right) + 1.5$$ $$\sigma \approx 2.13 + 1.5 = 3.63\sigma$$ (Each numerical step ≈ 1 mark = 3 marks total.) **Step 4 — Role Deployment Justification (4 marks):** | Metric | Value | Implication | |--------|-------|-------------| | DPMO | 33,333 | Severely poor quality (industry benchmark ~3.4 DPMO) | | Sigma | 3.63σ | Below world-class; requires immediate DMAIC intervention | **Role deployment:** - **Executive Leadership:** Must approve a Six Sigma initiative at the corporate level given the COPQ exposure. **(0.7 marks)** - **Champion:** Should sponsor a Black Belt-led project to reduce soldering defects; tie the project to PCB yield KPI. **(0.7 marks)** - **Master Black Belt:** Should mentor the Black Belt on DOE for reflow temperature profiling. **(0.7 marks)** - **Black Belt:** Lead the full DMAIC project; Measure → solder-paste inspection data; Analyze → regression on temperature/humidity; Improve → DOE on reflow profile; Control → SPC on solder-joint quality. **(0.7 marks)** - **Green Belts:** Line operators trained in DMAIC basics; collect hourly defect data. **(0.4 marks)** - **Yellow Belts:** Frontline assemblers flag anomalies during shift handover. **(0.4 marks)** - **Process Owner:** SMT cell manager — owns the post-project control plan and monitors Cp/Cpk monthly. **(0.4 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas (KTU Board Pattern):** > 1. **Listing roles without DMAIC linkage** — merely writing "Black Belts lead projects" earns ≤ 2 marks; examiners want DMAIC-phase-specific statements. > 2. **Confusing Champion with Master Black Belt** — Champions are *sponsors/managers*, not statisticians. MBBs are *methodologists*, not decision-makers. > 3. **RACI errors** — assigning two "A"s to a task, or no "A" at all, loses full marks for that row. > 4. **Numerical errors** — wrong conversion between DPO, DPMO, and σ. Memorise: $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}(\dots) + 1.5$ (1.5σ shift). > 5. **Skipping Process Owner** — students often omit this role. KTU examiners frequently award a dedicated 1 mark for correctly identifying the Process Owner's *Control* responsibility. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Six Sigma Roles Hierarchy (top → bottom):** Executive Leadership → Champions → Master Black Belts → Black Belts → Green Belts → Yellow Belts → Process Owners. - **Champion's charter:** Select, sponsor, sign-off, remove barriers. *Accountable* across DMAIC. - **Master Black Belt's charter:** Train, mentor, audit; expert-level statistician. - **Black Belt's charter:** Full-time project leader; *Responsible* for Measure→Improve. - **Green Belt's charter:** Part-time participant; bridges BB ↔ YB; leads smaller projects. - **Yellow Belt's charter:** Frontline awareness; supports Define + Control. - **Process Owner's charter:** Owns the process post-handover; *Accountable* for Control phase and sustainability. - **RACI:** Only **one Accountable** per task; multiple *Responsible*, *Consulted*, *Informed* allowed. - **DPMO formula:** $\dfrac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^{6}$ - **Sigma conversion:** $\sigma = \text{NORMSINV}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}(1 - \text{DPO}) + 0.5\right) + 1.5$ - **World-class target:** $\sigma \geq 6$ corresponds to **3.4 DPMO**. - **Project success criterion:** Sustained improvement owned by the **Process Owner**, not the Black Belt. - **Cultural note:** Yellow Belts and Green Belts ensure the *Kaizen mindset* spreads; without them, Six Sigma becomes a "Black Belt elite club" and dies out. - **KTU valuation tip:** Always end a 14-mark answer with a one-line **RACI table** or a **DMAIC-role matrix** to consolidate marks.

topics.title: Six Sigma in Manufacturing and Service Industries
Quality Management Systems: Benefits of ISO Registration
ISO 9001 Requirements – Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** ISO 9001 Requirements – Implementation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **ISO 9001:2015** is an internationally recognized **Quality Management System (QMS)** standard published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**. It specifies the generic requirements that an organization must fulfill to demonstrate its ability to consistently provide products and services that meet customer and applicable statutory/regulatory requirements, and aims to enhance customer satisfaction through the effective application of the QMS, including processes for improvement and the assurance of conformity to customer and applicable statutory/regulatory requirements. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, ISO 9001 implementation refers to the **systematic deployment of the 10 clauses (4 through 10)** of the standard within an organization, transforming documented procedures into operational practices supported by statistical quality control tools. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Restaurant Recipe Analogy for ISO 9001** > > Imagine you own a restaurant. To consistently serve delicious food: > - You need a **recipe book** (Quality Manual) > - Each recipe has **ingredients** (procedures) and **steps** (work instructions) > - Customers (internal and external) give **feedback** (audits, surveys) > - The chef (top management) ensures everything is followed (**Leadership**) > - You keep records of every dish (Records and Documentation) > > **ISO 9001 is the recipe book for the entire business.** It tells an organization *what* to do, *how* to document it, *how* to check it works, and *how* to improve it — continuously. Just as a recipe ensures every plate tastes the same in every branch of the restaurant chain, ISO 9001 ensures the quality of products/services remains consistent regardless of who produces them or where. ## 1.3 Why ISO 9001 Implementation Matters - **Global Credibility:** Certification acts as a passport in international trade, recognized in **over 190 countries**. - **Customer Confidence:** Reduces buyer risk and serves as a **pre-qualification criterion** for tenders (especially in aerospace, automotive, and medical sectors). - **Operational Efficiency:** The standard's process approach and risk-based thinking eliminate redundancies, saving industry an estimated **5–15%** of total operating cost (industry benchmarks). - **Statutory Compliance:** Aligns with regulatory expectations across sectors. ## 1.4 The 10 Clauses of ISO 9001:2015 (High-Level Map) | Clause Range | Title | Nature | |:------------:|:------|:-------| | 1 | Scope | Informative | | 2 | Normative References | Informative | | 3 | Terms and Definitions | Informative | | 4 | Context of the Organization | Requirement | | 5 | Leadership | Requirement | | 6 | Planning | Requirement | | 7 | Support | Requirement | | 8 | Operation | Requirement | | 9 | Performance Evaluation | Requirement | | 10 | Improvement | Requirement | > [!NOTE] > Clauses **4 through 10 are auditable** — meaning a third-party certification body (e.g., TUV SUD, BSI, Bureau Veritas) will verify compliance with these clauses. Clauses 1–3 are merely introductory and cannot fail an audit. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Seven Quality Management Principles (ISO's Foundation) The standard is built upon **seven universally accepted management principles**. Examiners frequently ask students to enumerate and explain these. | # | Principle | One-Line Meaning | |:-:|:----------|:-----------------| | 1 | **Customer Focus** | Meeting and exceeding customer expectations | | 2 | **Leadership** | Establishing unity of purpose and direction | | 3 | **Engagement of People** | Competent, empowered people at all levels | | 4 | **Process Approach** | Managing activities as interrelated processes | | 5 | **Improvement** | Continual improvement as a permanent objective | | 6 | **Evidence-Based Decision Making** | Decisions based on analysis of data | | 7 | **Relationship Management** | Managing relationships with interested parties | ## 2.2 The PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act) — The Engine of Implementation > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle, also known as the **Deming Wheel**, is the operational backbone of ISO 9001. Every process within the QMS must follow this cycle to ensure continual improvement. | Stage | Activity in ISO 9001 Context | |:------|:------------------------------| | **Plan** | Establish objectives, processes, and resources (Clauses 6, 7) | | **Do** | Implement the planned processes (Clause 8 — Operation) | | **Check** | Monitor, measure, analyze, and evaluate (Clause 9) | | **Act** | Take corrective actions and pursue improvement (Clause 10) | ## 2.3 Risk-Based Thinking (RBT) — ISO 9001:2015's Major Shift > [!NOTE] > A defining innovation of ISO 9001:2015 (replacing ISO 9001:2008) is the **explicit requirement of Risk-Based Thinking** at every process level. The previous requirement of "Preventive Action" has been replaced by RBT, requiring organizations to identify risks and opportunities proactively. **Risk Assessment Formula (used in operational planning):** $$ R = P \times S $$ Where: - $R$ = Risk Priority Number (Score) - $P$ = Probability (or Likelihood) of occurrence, typically scored 1–5 - $S$ = Severity (or Impact) of consequence, typically scored 1–5 > The corresponding **Risk Level Matrix** is: | Probability (P) ↓ \ Severity (S) → | **1 (Negligible)** | **2 (Minor)** | **3 (Moderate)** | **4 (Major)** | **5 (Catastrophic)** | |:----------------------------------:|:------------------:|:-------------:|:----------------:|:-------------:|:--------------------:| | **5 (Almost Certain)** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | **25** | | **4 (Likely)** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | **20** | | **3 (Possible)** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 | | **2 (Unlikely)** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 | | **1 (Rare)** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | > **Acceptance Threshold:** Generally, $R \ge 15$ demands **immediate mitigation actions**. ## 2.4 Documentation Hierarchy — The "Pyramid" of QMS Documents > [!IMPORTANT] > ISO 9001:2015 reduced the mandatory documentation requirement significantly compared to 2008. There are now only **two mandatory documented pieces of information**: (i) the scope of the QMS, and (ii) the Quality Policy and Objectives. However, **6 clauses require documented information** based on context. The typical QMS documentation follows this pyramid: ``` ┌─────────────────┐ │ QUALITY MANUAL │ (Clause 4.3) — Top-Level └─────────────────┘ │ ┌────────┴────────┐ │ PROCEDURES │ (Mandatory where stated) └─────────────────┘ │ ┌────────────┴────────────┐ │ WORK INSTRUCTIONS / │ (Process-specific) │ SOPs / METHODS │ └─────────────────────────┘ │ ┌────────────┴────────────┐ │ FORMS / RECORDS / │ (Objective Evidence) │ DATA / FILES │ └─────────────────────────┘ ``` ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet for SQC + ISO 9001 | Formula / Concept | Equation / Statement | Application | |:------------------|:---------------------|:------------| | **Risk Score** | $R = P \times S$ | Clause 6.1 (Actions to address risks) | | **Process Capability Index** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left( \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$ | Linking SQC tools (Module 3) to Clause 9 | | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good Units Produced}}{\text{Total Units Entering Process}}$ | Clause 9.1 (Monitoring & Measurement) | | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$ | Clause 9.1 — Performance Evaluation | | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Clause 8 — Operation | | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $COPQ = \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Prevention}$ | Clause 10 — Improvement | | **Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)** | $CSI = \dfrac{\text{Satisfied Responses}}{\text{Total Responses}} \times 100\%$ | Clause 9.1.2 | > **Boundary Condition:** A process is considered **statistically capable** if $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ (industry minimum) and **world-class** if $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Sector (IATF 16949, derived from ISO 9001):** Requires demonstrated PPAP, APQP, and FMEA — all rooted in ISO 9001's risk-based thinking. - **Medical Devices (ISO 13485):** Builds on ISO 9001's framework for regulatory traceability. - **Software (CMMI, ISO 25010):** Borrowed process approach and continual improvement philosophy. - **Aerospace (AS9100):** Extends ISO 9001 with safety-critical risk management. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The ISO 9001 Implementation Roadmap — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Below is the **canonical 12-step implementation methodology** used by consultancy firms worldwide (TÜV, BSI, BVQI) and frequently tested in KTU ESE. ### Step 1: Top Management Commitment The CEO/MD must issue a **Quality Policy Statement** and allocate resources (Clause 5.1). - Documented evidence: Board Resolution dated with budget allocation. ### Step 2: Define the Scope of the QMS (Clause 4.3) Specify boundaries, applicability, and exclusions. Justify any exclusion using **Clause 8** non-applicability logic. $$ \text{Scope} = \{ \text{Products}, \text{Services}, \text{Locations}, \text{Process Boundaries} \} $$ ### Step 3: Context of the Organization Analysis (Clause 4.1) Identify **internal issues** (culture, knowledge, infrastructure) and **external issues** (legal, technological, market, cultural) using **SWOT** or **PESTLE** analysis. ### Step 4: Needs and Expectations of Interested Parties (Clause 4.2) Construct a **Stakeholder Matrix**: $$ \text{Influence} \times \text{Interest} = \text{Engagement Strategy} $$ ### Step 5: Establish the Process Map (Clause 4.4) Every process requires: 1. **Inputs** — $I$ 2. **Outputs** — $O$ 3. **Responsibilities** — $R$ 4. **KPIs** — $K$ 5. **Risks/Opportunities** — $RO$ The acronym **SIRKO** summarizes process documentation: $$ \text{Process Card} = \{S, I, R, K, O\} $$ ### Step 6: Define Quality Policy and Objectives (Clause 5.2 & 6.2) **SMART Objectives:** $$ \text{SMART} = \{\text{Specific},\ \text{Measurable},\ \text{Achievable},\ \text{Relevant},\ \text{Time-bound}\} $$ **Cascading Objective Tree:** $$ O_{\text{Corporate}} \rightarrow O_{\text{Department}} \rightarrow O_{\text{Individual}} $$ ### Step 7: Risk Assessment (Clause 6.1) Compute $R = P \times S$ for every identified risk. $$ \text{Risk Treatment Options} = \{ \text{Avoid},\ \text{Mitigate},\ \text{Transfer},\ \text{Accept} \} $$ ### Step 8: Provide Resources and Competence (Clause 7) - Allocate infrastructure, environment, monitoring equipment. - Maintain **competence matrix** for every employee. $$ \text{Competence Gap} = \text{Required Skill Level} - \text{Current Skill Level} $$ If $\text{Gap} > 0$, **training is mandatory** and must be recorded. ### Step 9: Operational Planning and Control (Clause 8) Determine: - Product/service requirements - Design and development controls - Control of externally provided processes (Clause 8.4) - Production and service provision (Clause 8.5) - Release of products and services (Clause 8.6) ### Step 10: Monitoring, Measurement, Analysis, and Evaluation (Clause 9) Key activities: - **Customer Satisfaction Surveys** (Clause 9.1.2) - **Internal Audit** (Clause 9.2) — minimum annual frequency - **Management Review** (Clause 9.3) — minimum twice yearly The **Internal Audit Cycle**: $$ \text{Audit Planning} \rightarrow \text{Audit Execution} \rightarrow \text{NC Issuance} \rightarrow \text{CAR} \rightarrow \text{Verification} $$ Where NC = Non-Conformity and CAR = Corrective Action Request. ### Step 11: Nonconformity and Corrective Action (Clause 10.2) The **8D (Eight Disciplines) Problem-Solving** method: $$ D1 \rightarrow D2 \rightarrow D3 \rightarrow D4 \rightarrow D5 \rightarrow D6 \rightarrow D7 \rightarrow D8 $$ $$ \{\text{Team}, \text{Describe}, \text{Contain}, \text{Root Cause}, \text{Permanent Fix}, \text{Implement}, \text{Prevent}, \text{Recognize}\} $$ ### Step 12: Continual Improvement (Clause 10.3) Use tools such as: - Kaizen events - Six Sigma (DMAIC) - Lean (5S, Value Stream Mapping) - Statistical Process Control (from Module 3) $$ \text{Improvement Index} = \dfrac{KPI_{\text{current}} - KPI_{\text{baseline}}}{KPI_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100\% $$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Linking Statistical Tools to ISO 9001 Clauses | Statistical Tool (SQC) | ISO 9001 Clause Mapped | Purpose | |:-----------------------|:-----------------------|:--------| | Control Charts (X̄, R, p, c) | Clause 9.1.1 | Process monitoring | | Pareto Analysis | Clause 9.1.3 | Analysis & evaluation | | Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa) | Clause 10.2 | Root cause analysis | | Histogram / Normal Distribution | Clause 9.1.1 | Data analysis | | Acceptance Sampling (ANSI/ASQ Z1.4) | Clause 8.6 | Product release | | FMEA (Failure Mode & Effects Analysis) | Clause 6.1, 8.3 | Design control + risk | | Regression / Correlation | Clause 9.1.3 | Decision-making | | Cpk, Pp, Ppk Indices | Clause 9.1.1 | Capability assessment | ## 3.3 Numerical Example — Risk Score Computation (Worked Out) **Problem Statement:** A manufacturing process has a Probability rating of **4** (Likely) and a Severity rating of **3** (Moderate). Compute the Risk Priority Number. **Solution:** $$ R = P \times S $$ $$ R = 4 \times 3 = 12 $$ Referring to the Risk Level Matrix in Section 2.3, $R = 12$ falls in the **Medium Risk** zone (yellow zone). Per the acceptance threshold of $R \ge 15$, no immediate mitigation is legally mandated, but a **monitoring plan** is recommended. **Decision Rule:** $$ \text{Action} = \begin{cases} \text{Immediate Mitigation}, & R \ge 15 \\ \text{Periodic Review}, & 8 \le R < 15 \\ \text{Accept and Monitor}, & R < 8 \end{cases} $$ ## 3.4 Numerical Example — Customer Satisfaction Index **Problem:** A company received **437 positive responses** out of **500** total customer surveys in Q3. $$ CSI = \dfrac{437}{500} \times 100\% = 87.4\% $$ Compared with the Q1 baseline of $80\%$, the **Improvement Index** is: $$ \text{Improvement Index} = \dfrac{87.4 - 80.0}{80.0} \times 100\% = 9.25\% $$ This positive trend provides objective evidence for **Management Review** (Clause 9.3). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — ISO 9001 Implementation Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TD A[startA[Top Management Commitment]] --> B[startB[Define QMS Scope]] B --> C[startC[Context Analysis SWOT PESTLE]] C --> D[startD[Identify Interested Parties] D --> E[startE[Build Process Map] E --> F[startF[Set Quality Objectives] F --> G[startG[Risk Assessment] G --> H[startH[Resource Allocation and Training] H --> I[startI[Operational Control] I --> J[startJ[Monitoring and Internal Audit] J --> K[startK[Management Review] K --> L[startL[Nonconformity and CAR] L --> M[startM[Continual Improvement] M --> J M --> N[startN[Certification Audit] N --> O[startO[Continuous Surveillance] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — PDCA Cycle within ISO 9001:2015 ```mermaid flowchart LR P[nodeP[PLAN
Clauses 6 and 7
Objectives, Risks, Resources]] D[nodeD[DO
Clause 8
Execute Processes]] C[nodeC[CHECK
Clause 9
Monitor and Audit]] A1[nodeA[ACT
Clause 10
Improve and Correct]] P --> D D --> C C --> A1 A1 --> P ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — QMS Documentation Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TD top1[nodeTop[LEVEL 1: QUALITY MANUAL
Scope, Policy, Process Map]] proc1[nodeProc[LEVEL 2: MANDATORY PROCEDURES
Document Control, Internal Audit, NCR, CAPA]] sop1[nodeSop[LEVEL 3: WORK INSTRUCTIONS AND SOPs
Process Specific Methods]] form1[nodeForm[LEVEL 4: RECORDS AND FORMS
Objective Evidence and Data]] top1 --> proc1 proc1 --> sop1 sop1 --> form1 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Risk-Based Thinking Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD r1[nodeR1[Identify Process]] r2[nodeR2[Identify Risks and Opportunities]] r3[nodeR3[Assess Risk Score R equals P times S]] r4[nodeR4{R Greater than 15?} r5[nodeR5[Implement Mitigation Controls] r6[nodeR6[Accept and Monitor] r7[nodeR7[Review Effectiveness in Management Review] r1 --> r2 r2 --> r3 r3 --> r4 r4 -- YES --> r5 r4 -- NO --> r6 r5 --> r7 r6 --> r7 r7 --> r1 ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Certification Audit Stages ```mermaid flowchart LR s1[nodeS1[Stage 1 Audit
Documentation Review]] s2[nodeS2[Stage 2 Audit
On-site Implementation]] s3[nodeS3[Nonconformity Closure]] s4[nodeS4[Certificate Issuance
3 Years Validity]] s5[nodeS5[Surveillance Audit 1
12 Months]] s6[nodeS6[Surveillance Audit 2
24 Months]] s7[nodeS7[Recertification
36 Months]] s1 --> s2 s2 --> s3 s3 --> s4 s4 --> s5 s5 --> s6 s6 --> s7 s7 --> s5 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define ISO 9001 and state its significance in modern manufacturing industries.** **Model Answer:** ISO 9001 is an internationally recognized standard for **Quality Management Systems (QMS)**, published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO). The current version, ISO 9001:2015, specifies the requirements an organization must fulfill to consistently provide products and services that meet customer and regulatory requirements and to enhance customer satisfaction. **Significance:** 1. Provides a globally accepted framework for quality management. 2. Acts as a pre-qualification criterion in international tenders. 3. Improves operational efficiency via process approach and PDCA cycle. 4. Reduces cost of poor quality through evidence-based decision-making. 5. Enhances customer trust and brand reputation. *(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 2 marks significance points)* --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Explain the term "Risk-Based Thinking" as introduced in ISO 9001:2015.** **Model Answer:** Risk-Based Thinking (RBT) is a mandatory conceptual requirement introduced in **ISO 9001:2015**, replacing the older "Preventive Action" clause. It requires every organization to proactively **identify, assess, and address risks and opportunities** at the process level rather than reactively correcting defects. The risk score is computed as: $$ R = P \times S $$ where $P$ is the probability of occurrence and $S$ is the severity. A higher $R$ requires stronger mitigation controls. RBT ensures **preventive culture**, supports continual improvement, and aligns QMS objectives with organizational strategic goals. *(3 marks — 2 marks concept + 1 mark formula application)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > **INSTRUCTIONS:** Answer **ONE** full question. Each question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) Explain in detail the Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle. How does it integrate with the structure of ISO 9001:2015?** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** The **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle, also called the **Deming Cycle**, is a four-stage iterative management method used for the continual improvement of processes and products. ISO 9001:2015 mandates its application at every level of the QMS. **Stage 1 — PLAN (Establish the QMS):** - Identify context of the organization (Clause 4.1). - Determine interested parties (Clause 4.2). - Define scope, quality policy, and objectives (Clauses 4.3, 5.2, 6.2). - Identify risks and opportunities (Clause 6.1). - Plan resource allocation and competence (Clause 7). **Stage 2 — DO (Implement the QMS):** - Operational planning and control (Clause 8). - Control of production and service provision. - Design and development controls (Clause 8.3). - Release of products and services (Clause 8.6). **Stage 3 — CHECK (Monitor and Evaluate):** - Monitoring, measurement, analysis, and evaluation (Clause 9.1). - Customer satisfaction monitoring (Clause 9.1.2). - Internal audits (Clause 9.2). - Management review (Clause 9.3). **Stage 4 — ACT (Improve):** - Nonconformity and corrective action (Clause 10.2). - Continual improvement (Clause 10.3). **Integration Summary:** | PDCA Stage | ISO 9001 Clauses | |:-----------|:------------------| | Plan | 4, 5, 6, 7 | | Do | 8 | | Check | 9 | | Act | 10 | > The cycle repeats indefinitely, driving the QMS toward maturity. *(7 marks — 1 mark per stage + 2 marks for table)* --- **(b) Discuss the documentation requirements under ISO 9001:2015. Highlight the difference from the 2008 version.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** ISO 9001:2015 uses the term **"Documented Information"** instead of "documents" and "records" used in ISO 9001:2008. **Mandatory Documented Information (Clause 4–10):** 1. **Scope of the QMS** (Clause 4.3). 2. **Quality Policy** (Clause 5.2). 3. **Quality Objectives** (Clause 6.2). 4. **Criteria for evaluation and selection of suppliers** (Clause 8.4.1). 5. **Evidence of competence** of personnel (Clause 7.2). 6. **Evidence of process monitoring, measurement, and audits** (Clause 9). 7. **Management Review results** (Clause 9.3). 8. **Nature of nonconformities and corrective actions** (Clause 10.2). **Differences from 2008 Version:** | Aspect | ISO 9001:2008 | ISO 9001:2015 | |:-------|:---------------|:---------------| | Term used | Documents and Records | Documented Information | | Mandatory procedures | 6 (Quality Manual, NCR, CAPA, etc.) | Reduced — only 2 mandatory documents | | Preventive Action | Required separately | Replaced by Risk-Based Thinking | | Quality Manual | Mandatory | Optional (recommended but not required) | | Context of Organization | Not present | Explicit requirement (Clause 4.1) | | Leadership | Management Responsibility | Stronger focus on Leadership (Clause 5) | The 2015 version provides **flexibility**, allowing organizations to retain documentation suited to their size and complexity. *(7 marks — 3 marks mandatory list + 4 marks comparison)* --- ### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a) Describe the seven Quality Management Principles of ISO 9001:2015 with examples.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** The seven QMS principles form the philosophical foundation of ISO 9001:2015: | # | Principle | Description | Example | |:-:|:----------|:------------|:--------| | 1 | **Customer Focus** | Primary focus on meeting customer requirements and exceeding expectations | Conducting annual customer satisfaction surveys; on-time delivery KPI | | 2 | **Leadership** | Leaders establish unity of purpose, direction, and engagement | CEO communicating quality policy in town halls | | 3 | **Engagement of People** | Competent, empowered, and valued people at all levels | Cross-functional Kaizen teams | | 4 | **Process Approach** | Consistent and predictable results through managed processes | SIPOC diagrams; process flowcharts | | 5 | **Improvement** | Continual improvement as a permanent objective | PDCA cycle; Six Sigma DMAIC projects | | 6 | **Evidence-Based Decision Making** | Decisions based on data and information analysis | Control charts; SPC dashboards | | 7 | **Relationship Management** | Managing relationships with suppliers, partners, and society | Long-term supplier agreements; vendor scorecards | These principles are **not auditable** by themselves but guide the interpretation and implementation of the standard's requirements. *(7 marks — 1 mark per principle or 0.5 mark per principle + 0.5 mark example)* --- **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the steps involved in the implementation of ISO 9001 in an organization.** *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** The implementation follows a structured 10-step approach (simplified for KTU examination): 1. **Top Management Commitment** — Issue Quality Policy, allocate resources. 2. **Define Scope** — Specify products, services, and boundaries. 3. **Context Analysis** — SWOT, PESTLE, and stakeholder mapping. 4. **Process Mapping** — Identify core, support, and management processes. 5. **Document the QMS** — Quality Manual, procedures, SOPs, records. 6. **Risk Assessment** — Compute $R = P \times S$ for each process. 7. **Training and Awareness** — Ensure competence at all levels. 8. **Internal Audit** — Conduct first-party audits to assess readiness. 9. **Management Review** — Top management evaluates QMS performance. 10. **Certification Audit** — Engage third-party certification body. **Block Diagram of Implementation Process:** ```mermaid flowchart TD step1[step1[Top Management Commitment]] step2[step2[Define QMS Scope]] step3[step3[Context Analysis]] step4[step4[Process Mapping]] step5[step5[Documentation]] step6[step6[Risk Assessment]] step7[step7[Training and Awareness]] step8[step8[Internal Audit]] step9[step9[Management Review]] step10[step10[Certification Audit]] step1 --> step2 step2 --> step3 step3 --> step4 step4 --> step5 step5 --> step6 step6 --> step7 step7 --> step8 step8 --> step9 step9 --> step10 ``` > **Note:** The implementation is **iterative**, not linear. Each step feeds back into continual improvement (PDCA cycle). *(7 marks — 5 marks for explanation + 2 marks for diagram)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in ISO 9001 Questions (Based on KTU Valuation Patterns):** > > 1. **Clause Number Mix-Ups:** Students frequently confuse Clause 9 (Performance Evaluation) with Clause 10 (Improvement). Remember: **Check = 9, Act = 10**. > 2. **Version Confusion:** Writing ISO 9001:2008 requirements (e.g., separate Preventive Action clause) when the question specifies 2015 version will cost **2–3 marks** immediately. > 3. **Skipping the Formula:** For Risk-Based Thinking, merely saying "identify risks" without writing $R = P \times S$ loses **1 mark**. > 4. **Ignoring the Process Approach:** KTU examiners expect the SIPOC/process approach to be referenced in any implementation-related question. > 5. **No Diagram:** Part (b) of a 7-mark question without a labeled diagram forfeits **up to 2 marks** as per valuation key. > 6. **Confusing Audit Types:** First-party (internal), Second-party (supplier/customer), and Third-party (certification body) audits — clearly stating the type matters. > 7. **Forgetting Documented Evidence:** Every process needs objective evidence — explicitly mention "records", "logs", or "data" wherever applicable. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for ISO 9001:2015 Implementation:** > > - ISO 9001:2015 has **10 clauses**; **Clauses 4–10 are auditable**. > - **PDCA cycle** maps as: Plan (4–7), Do (8), Check (9), Act (10). > - **Seven QMS principles** — know all seven with one industrial example each. > - **Risk-Based Thinking** replaced Preventive Action; compute $R = P \times S$. > - **Mandatory documented information:** Scope + Quality Policy + Objectives. > - **Quality Manual** is **optional** in 2015 (mandatory in 2008). > - **Process Approach** uses **SIPOC** — Supplier, Input, Process, Output, Customer. > - **Internal audit** must be conducted at least **once a year**. > - **Management Review** must be conducted at least **twice a year**. > - **Certification validity:** 3 years, with **surveillance audits** at 12 and 24 months. > - **3 audit types:** First-party (internal), Second-party (supplier), Third-party (certification). > - **Statistical tools mapping:** Control charts → Clause 9.1, FMEA → Clause 6.1/8.3, Pareto → Clause 9.1.3. > - **8D Problem-Solving** method — used for Clause 10.2 corrective actions. > - **Cpk ≥ 1.33** is the industry minimum capability threshold — frequently quoted. > - For 14-mark answers, always include **at least one table and one diagram** for full marks.

topics.title: Sector-Specific Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEIET611 - STATISTICAL QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sector-Specific Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SECTOR-SPECIFIC STANDARDS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sector-Specific Standards** are formalized, industry-tailored quality management frameworks, certifications, and technical specifications that supplement generic quality standards (such as **ISO 9001**) with additional requirements addressing the unique risks, regulatory obligations, technical characteristics, and customer expectations of a particular industrial or service sector.

In the context of **Statistical Quality Control (SQC)**, these standards dictate *which quality tools must be applied, how process capability indices are interpreted, what acceptance sampling plans are permissible, and how measurement systems must be validated* within a given industry.

The major classifications under KTU 2024 Module 4 include:

| Acronym | Full Form | Industry Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **IATF 16949** | International Automotive Task Force | Automotive Manufacturing |
| **AS9100** | Aerospace Standard 9100 | Aerospace & Defense |
| **ISO 13485** | International Organization for Standardization 13485 | Medical Devices |
| **ISO 22000** | ISO Food Safety Management | Food & Beverage |
| **HACCP** | Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points | Food Safety |
| **cGMP** | Current Good Manufacturing Practice | Pharmaceuticals |
| **TL 9000** | Telecommunications Quality | Telecom / ICT |
| **CMMI** | Capability Maturity Model Integration | Software / IT Services |
| **ISO/IEC 17025** | ISO/IEC Testing & Calibration Labs | Testing Laboratories |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving License" Metaphor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box:** Think of a **generic driving license (ISO 9001)** as the universal permit to drive. It certifies that you know the *basic rules of the road* — stop at red lights, signal before turning, maintain the vehicle. Now imagine you want to drive a **heavy truck carrying explosives**, a **school bus full of children**, or a **commercial passenger aircraft**. Will a *basic* license be enough? Obviously not. You need **extra certifications** — special endorsements, hazardous-materials training, type-rating for the aircraft.
>
> **Sector-Specific Standards are exactly these "endorsements"** bolted onto the generic quality license. They exist because a *pharmaceutical tablet* and a *toy car* are both "products," but the risks of failure are wildly different, so the statistical controls, traceability requirements, and audit rigour must also differ.

### 1.3 Why Generic Standards Are Not Sufficient

Generic standards like **ISO 9001** operate on the principle of *"say what you do, do what you say, prove it."* They are **process-oriented** and **risk-based**, but they do not prescribe:
- Specific SQC tools (e.g., FMEA, SPC, MSA)
- Acceptable defect rates per million opportunities (DPMO)
- Sector-specific documentation (e.g., Device History Record in medical devices)
- Industry-mandated customer satisfaction metrics

Hence, **sector-specific standards fill this prescriptive gap**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 Theoretical Pillars of Sector-Specific Standards

The foundation of every sector-specific quality standard rests on four pillars. Understanding these helps in **comparative analysis questions** in the KTU ESE.

### Pillar 1 — Risk-Based Thinking Amplification
- **Generic ISO 9001:** Risk management is implied.
- **Sector-specific:** Risk is *quantified* (e.g., **Risk Priority Number, RPN** in IATF 16949; **FMEA Severity × Occurrence × Detection** in AS9100).
- **SQC Implication:** Drives the choice of **control chart sensitivity** and **sampling plan stringency**.

### Pillar 2 — Traceability and Documentation
- Medical devices → **Device History Record (DHR)** per **ISO 13485**.
- Pharmaceuticals → **Batch records** under **cGMP (21 CFR Part 211)**.
- Food → **Lot tracking and recall capability** under **ISO 22000**.
- **SQC Implication:** Every measurement data point must be **traceable to the operator, machine, raw material batch, and time-stamp**.

### Pillar 3 — Measurement System Assurance
- **IATF 16949** mandates **MSA (Measurement System Analysis)** with strict acceptance criteria:
  - **GRR (Gauge Repeatability & Reproducibility):** $\vert \text{GRR}\% \vert < 10\%$ — excellent; $10\% - 30\%$ — marginal; $> 30\%$ — unacceptable.
  - **ndc (Number of Distinct Categories):** $\geq 5$.
- **AS9100** extends this to **calibration to national standards (NIST, NPL)** with uncertainty budgets.
- **SQC Implication:** No SPC chart is valid until the measurement system is proven capable.

### Pillar 4 — Customer-Specific Requirements (CSR)
- Automakers (Ford, GM, Toyota) publish **CSR documents** that override generic IATF clauses.
- Telecom operators mandate **TL 9000 measurements handbook** performance metrics.
- **SQC Implication:** Defines the **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** characteristics and their specification limits.

## 2.2 KTU Reference Matrix — Sector Standards Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **high-yield** for KTU ESE Part A (3-mark) comparative questions. Note: vertical bars in formulas use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Feature | ISO 9001 (Generic) | IATF 16949 (Auto) | ISO 13485 (Medical) | AS9100 (Aerospace) | ISO 22000 (Food) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Year (latest)** | 2015 | 2016 | 2016 | 2016 (Rev D) | 2018 |
| **Risk Tool** | Context-based | **FMEA + RPN** | Risk Mgmt File | **FMEA mandatory** | **HACCP plan** |
| **MSA Required** | Optional | **Mandatory (AIAG)** | Mandatory | **Mandatory (Rigorous)** | Indirect |
| **PPAP / DFM** | No | **Yes (PPAP)** | Design History File | **FAI (First Article Inspection)** | N/A |
| **SPC Mandate** | Recommended | **Mandatory for CTQ** | Strongly recommended | **Mandatory (Critical chars)** | CCP monitoring |
| **Defect Metric** | Internal PPM | **DPMO / PPM (CSR)** | Complaints / Recall | **DPMO + AS9145** | Per lot rejection |
| **Audit Focus** | Process | **Process + Product** | Product Safety | **Product + Configuration** | Hazard control |
| **Certificate Validity** | 3 years | 3 years (surveillance) | 3 years | 3 years | 3 years |

## 2.3 The Statistical Quality Control Layer Inside Sector Standards

While standards prescribe *what* must be done, **SQC techniques provide the *how***. The integration looks like this:

$$\text{Sector Standard} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{Risk Tool (FMEA)} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \text{SQC Technique}$$

Mapping examples (frequently asked in KTU):

| SQC Tool | Sector Application | Standard |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Charts ($\bar{X}, R, p, c$)** | Process monitoring of CTQ | IATF 16949, AS9100 |
| **Process Capability ($C_p, C_{pk}$)** | Supplier qualification | IATF 16949 ($\geq 1.33$) |
| **Acceptance Sampling (AQL, LTPD, AOQL)** | Incoming inspection | ISO 2859-1, IATF |
| **Gauge R&R (GRR)** | MSA studies | IATF, AS9100, ISO 13485 |
| **Pareto Analysis** | Defect prioritization | All sectors |
| **Six-Sigma ($6\sigma$ DPMO)** | World-class quality | Motorola-derived, applied across |
| **DOE (Design of Experiments)** | Robust design | AS9100, IATF (APQP) |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

For a KTU 2024 B.Tech student, the practical utility of studying sector-specific standards includes:

1. **Placement Interviews:** Tier-1 automotive (Tata Motors, Bosch), aerospace (HAL, Boeing suppliers), and medical device firms (Stryker, GE Healthcare) **explicitly test knowledge of IATF, AS9100, and ISO 13485** in Quality/Operations roles.
2. **Final-Year Projects:** Projects involving process improvement on campus workshops (CNC, food tech, biotech) gain **higher publication and industry-acceptance value** when benchmarked against sector standards.
3. **Internship Reports:** Companies require mapping of student project work to specific standard clauses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedure for Standard Selection & Gap Analysis

This section provides the **exhaustive analytical procedure** that examiners expect for a 14-mark question. Every step is expanded with full justification.

## 3.1 Problem Framework — "How does a firm select and implement the right sector-specific standard?"

The selection and implementation follows a **five-stage gated methodology**. The procedure is purely analytical — no code is involved (per the SQC subject domain constraint).

### Stage 1 — Sector Identification and Scope Definition

**Step 1.1:** Identify the **primary industry sector** of the organization.

$$\text{Sector} = \arg\max_{i} \left( \text{Revenue Share}_i \right)$$

where $i$ ranges over candidate industry classifications. For multi-product firms, the sector with the **dominant revenue share** drives the primary certification, while secondary sectors may require **integrated audits** (e.g., IATF + ISO 13485 for an automotive medical device).

**Step 1.2:** Identify the **regulatory authority** governing the geography of sale:
- **USA:** FDA (Food, Drug, Medical), FAA (Aerospace), EPA (Environmental)
- **EU:** CE Marking, EASA, EMA
- **India:** BIS, CDSCO (medical), FSSAI (food), DGCA (aerospace)
- **Japan:** PMDA (pharma), JCAB (aviation)

**Step 1.3:** Identify the **customer-mandated requirements**:
- Many OEMs (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Boeing) **dictate** which certifications a supplier must hold. These are called **Customer Specific Requirements (CSRs)** and take precedence over generic clauses.

### Stage 2 — Gap Analysis Procedure

The standard procedure for **gap analysis** is as follows:

**Step 2.1:** Construct a **compliance matrix** with rows = standard clauses and columns = (a) Current state, (b) Required state, (c) Gap.

$$
\text{Gap}_{i} = \text{Required State}_{i} - \text{Current State}_{i}
$$

**Step 2.2:** Compute the **gap closure priority index** $P_i$ for each non-compliant clause:

$$
P_i = w_{r,i} \cdot R_i + w_{c,i} \cdot C_i + w_{d,i} \cdot D_i
$$

where:
- $R_i$ = Risk severity score (1–10) of the gap
- $C_i$ = Customer-audit criticality (1–10)
- $D_i$ = Difficulty of closure (1–10)
- $w_{r,i} + w_{c,i} + w_{d,i} = 1$ (normalized weights)

**Step 2.3:** Rank clauses by $P_i$ descending. Top $20\%$ typically receive **immediate CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action)** resources.

### Stage 3 — SQC Tool Mapping (Clause-by-Clause)

For each *missing* or *weak* clause, the SQC tool to be deployed is identified using the **mapping table** from Section 2.3.

**Example derivation:** Suppose an automotive tier-1 supplier is preparing for **IATF 16949** certification. The clause **IATF 16949:2016, §8.5.6.1 (Control of changes)** requires documented change control with risk assessment.

The analytical mapping is:

$$
\text{Change Request (CR)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{FMEA} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{RPN} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Decision}
$$

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

where:
- $S$ = Severity (1–10)
- $O$ = Occurrence (1–10)
- $D$ = Detection (1–10)
- $\text{RPN}_{\text{threshold}} = 100$ (typical IATF action trigger; values > 100 require immediate mitigation)

**Step 3.1:** For each process step affected by the change, compute $\text{RPN}_{\text{pre}}$ (before) and $\text{RPN}_{\text{post}}$ (after mitigation):

$$
\Delta \text{RPN} = \text{RPN}_{\text{pre}} - \text{RPN}_{\text{post}}
$$

**Step 3.2:** Acceptance criterion:

$$
\frac{\Delta \text{RPN}}{\text{RPN}_{\text{pre}}} \times 100\% \;\geq\; 50\%
$$

indicates effective risk reduction, satisfying the IATF clause.

### Stage 4 — Process Capability Verification

For **CTQ characteristics** identified under the sector standard, the SQC procedure is:

**Step 4.1:** Collect $n \geq 25$ subgroups of size $m = 5$ (or as per standard).

**Step 4.2:** Compute sample mean and standard deviation:

$$
\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{j=1}^{k} \bar{X}_j
$$

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{j=1}^{k} R_j
$$

**Step 4.3:** Estimate $\hat{\sigma}$ from $\bar{R}$:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2(m)}
$$

where $d_2(5) = 2.326$ (standard constant for subgroup size 5).

**Step 4.4:** Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$:

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}}
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min \left( \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{\bar{X}}}{3\hat{\sigma}}, \frac{\bar{\bar{X}} - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}} \right)
$$

**Step 4.5:** Apply sector-specific acceptance:
- **IATF 16949 (Initial):** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for CTQ.
- **IATF 16949 (Safety / Regulatory):** $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$.
- **AS9100 (Critical):** $C_{pk} \geq 1.50$.
- **Six-Sigma target (world class):** $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$ ($6\sigma$ within $\pm 6\sigma$).

If $C_{pk} < \text{target}$, the process is **not capable**, and the standard requires documented corrective action, not merely identification.

### Stage 5 — Audit, Surveillance, and Continual Improvement

**Step 5.1:** Internal audits follow the **PDCA (Plan–Do–Check–Act)** cycle as mandated by all sector standards.

**Step 5.2:** External certification body conducts **Stage 1 (Documentation)** and **Stage 2 (Implementation)** audits.

**Step 5.3:** Post-certification, **surveillance audits** occur at 6-month or 12-month intervals. **Non-conformities (NCs)** trigger CAPA with deadlines:
- **Major NC:** 60 days
- **Minor NC:** 90 days

**Step 5.4:** **Recertification** is mandatory every 3 years.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Exam Writing:** Always mention the **PDCA cycle** and **risk-based thinking** in any 7-mark sector standards answer. These are *guaranteed scoring keywords* as per the KTU 2024 scheme.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Illustration

**Problem:** A CNC machining firm (automotive tier-1) measures shaft diameter. From 25 subgroups of size 5:
- $\bar{\bar{X}} = 25.02$ mm
- $\bar{R} = 0.08$ mm
- Specification: LSL = 24.90 mm, USL = 25.10 mm

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $\hat{\sigma}$:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2(5)} = \frac{0.08}{2.326} = 0.0344 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $C_p$:

$$
C_p = \frac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.0344} = \frac{0.20}{0.2064} = 0.969
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $C_{pk}$:

$$
C_{pk, \text{upper}} = \frac{25.10 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.0344} = \frac{0.08}{0.1032} = 0.775
$$

$$
C_{pk, \text{lower}} = \frac{25.02 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.0344} = \frac{0.12}{0.1032} = 1.163
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(0.775, \; 1.163) = 0.775
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation under IATF 16949:
- $C_{pk} = 0.775 < 1.33$ → **Process is NOT capable** for IATF CTQ acceptance.
- Mean is **off-centre** (note: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 25.02$ is closer to USL than LSL, hence the asymmetry in $C_{pk}$ upper/lower values).
- **Required action:** Re-centre the machine, reduce variation ($\hat{\sigma}$), and re-validate.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sector-Standard Hierarchy — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **hierarchical relationship** between the generic ISO 9001 base and the sector-specific extensions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ISO 9001:2015 Generic QMS Base] --> B[Sector-Specific Extensions Layer]
    B --> C[IATF 16949 - Automotive]
    B --> D[AS9100 - Aerospace]
    B --> E[ISO 13485 - Medical Devices]
    B --> F[ISO 22000 - Food Safety]
    B --> G[TL 9000 - Telecom]
    B --> H[CMMI - Software]
    C --> C1[FMEA / APQP / PPAP / MSA / SPC]
    D --> D1[FAI / Configuration Mgmt / FAI / NDT]
    E --> E1[Design History File / Risk Mgmt File / DHR]
    F --> F1[HACCP Plan / CCP Monitoring / PRP / OPRP]
    G --> G1[TL Measurements / Network Performance]
    H --> H1[Process Areas / Maturity Levels 1-5]
    C1 --> I[SQC Core Tools]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
> **Distinguish between ISO 9001 and IATF 16949. Why is a generic standard insufficient for the automotive industry?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **ISO 9001** is a *generic, process-based QMS standard* applicable to any organization; it prescribes *what* must be done but not the *tools*.
- **IATF 16949** is a *sector-specific automotive extension*; it mandates specific SQC tools — **APQP, PPAP, FMEA, MSA, SPC, and Control Plans** — and requires **Customer Specific Requirements (CSRs)** to be addressed.
- Generic ISO 9001 is insufficient for automotive because it does not mandate **process capability ($C_{pk} \geq 1.33$)**, **PPAP submission**, or **supplier development protocols** demanded by global OEMs for safety-critical parts.
- (1 mark each for distinction points, total 3 marks.)

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
> **Define RPN. Why is it central to IATF 16949 and AS9100 risk management?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **RPN (Risk Priority Number)** is defined as:

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

- where $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), $D$ = Detection (1–10).
- RPN is central because both **IATF 16949** and **AS9100** mandate **FMEA-based risk assessment** for every process change and new product introduction.
- A high RPN ($\geq 100$) triggers **mandatory corrective action** and is auditable evidence of *risk-based thinking*, satisfying the **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 6.1** requirement.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Important:** As per KTU 2024 scheme, Module 4 will offer an internal choice. **Answer ANY ONE** of the following.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Sector Standards Treatment

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

> **(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **hierarchy of sector-specific standards** built on ISO 9001. Discuss **any three** sector standards in detail, highlighting the specific SQC tools each mandates. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A medical device manufacturer in Kerala seeks **ISO 13485** certification. Outline the **step-by-step implementation methodology** including sector-specific documentation, risk management, and statistical process control. Show how $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is verified for a critical dimension of a syringe barrel. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (A-a):

**(i) Hierarchy explanation (3 marks):**
The hierarchy is *ISO 9001 (Generic Base) → Sector-Specific Extensions (IATF 16949, AS9100, ISO 13485, ISO 22000) → SQC Core Tools (SPC, MSA, FMEA, Sampling, DOE) → Process Outputs (PPM, DPMO, Customer Satisfaction)*. The generic base provides the QMS skeleton; the sector-specific layer prescribes *tools*; the SQC layer provides *measurement and control*.

**(ii) Three sector standards (4 marks):**

- **IATF 16949 (Automotive):** Mandates **APQP, PPAP, FMEA, MSA, SPC, Control Plans**. Requires $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for CTQ features, and $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ for safety-critical features. Customer Specific Requirements (CSRs) override generic clauses.
- **AS9100 (Aerospace):** Mandates **FAI (First Article Inspection), Configuration Management, FOD (Foreign Object Debris) prevention, and NDT traceability**. Risk management is via AS9145 (RPN-based). Counterfeit Parts Prevention (AS5553) is a unique aerospace clause.
- **ISO 13485 (Medical Devices):** Mandates **Design History File (DHF), Device Master Record (DMR), Device History Record (DHR), Risk Management File (per ISO 14971)**, and post-market surveillance. Software validation (per IEC 62304) and usability engineering (IEC 62366) are included.

#### Model Solution (A-b):

**Step 1 — Context & Scope (1 mark):** Identify product family (syringe barrel), regulatory class (Class IIa/IIb in India per CDSCO), and applicable harmonized standards (ISO 13485 + ISO 14971 + ISO 7886-1 for syringes).

**Step 2 — Risk Management (1.5 marks):** Conduct FMEA on the barrel extrusion process. For a critical dimension (e.g., inner diameter affecting flow rate):

$$
\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D
$$

A RPN > 100 requires CAPA; the mitigation includes tighter process control and MSA.

**Step 3 — SPC Implementation (1.5 marks):** Install $\bar{X}-R$ chart. Collect 25 subgroups of size 5. Compute control limits:

$$
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \quad \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}
$$

For $n=5$, $A_2 = 0.577$.

**Step 4 — Process Capability Verification (2 marks):**

Given for the syringe barrel: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 4.005$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.015$ mm, USL = 4.030 mm, LSL = 3.970 mm.

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{0.015}{2.326} = 0.00645 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
C_p = \frac{4.030 - 3.970}{6 \times 0.00645} = \frac{0.060}{0.0387} = 1.55
$$

$$
C_{pk, \text{upper}} = \frac{4.030 - 4.005}{3 \times 0.00645} = \frac{0.025}{0.01935} = 1.292
$$

$$
C_{pk, \text{lower}} = \frac{4.005 - 3.970}{3 \times 0.00645} = \frac{0.035}{0.01935} = 1.809
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.292, 1.809) = 1.292
$$

**Interpretation:** $C_{pk} = 1.292 < 1.33$ → **Just below acceptance**. The mean is shifted toward USL. **Action:** Re-centre the extrusion line, reduce $\bar{R}$ via tooling upgrade, then re-validate. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Documentation & Audit (1 mark):** Compile DHF, DMR, DHR. Conduct internal audit (PDCA), followed by Stage 1 (documentation) and Stage 2 (implementation) audit by a notified body (e.g., TÜV SÜD, BSI). Obtain ISO 13485 certificate (3-year validity).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **statistical quality control requirements of AS9100 (Aerospace)**. How does configuration management and FAI integrate with SQC tools? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An aerospace component (titanium bracket) has specification LSL = 12.00 mm, USL = 12.10 mm. Twenty-five subgroups of size 5 gave $\bar{\bar{X}} = 12.062$ mm and $\bar{R} = 0.024$ mm. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ and comment on AS9100 compliance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (B-a):

**(i) AS9100 SQC Requirements (3 marks):**
- **Configuration Management:** Every design baseline is uniquely identified; changes follow formal **ECN (Engineering Change Notice)** with FMEA re-evaluation.
- **FAI (First Article Inspection):** Per AS9102, the first production piece of every new design or post-change lot is inspected against 100% of design characteristics with full traceability.
- **Risk Management:** AS9145 mandates **RPN**-based FMEA; AS6081 for counterfeit parts prevention; AS5553 for electronic components.
- **NDT Integration:** Non-Destructive Testing results are tracked and correlated with SQC data for trend analysis.

**(ii) FAI & SQC Integration (2 marks):** FAI verifies that the *first* piece meets all design characteristics. SPC then monitors *subsequent* production. Any out-of-control signal in SPC triggers a re-FAI for the affected lot.

**(iii) Tool Integration (2 marks):** AS9100 mandates **MSA (Gauge R&R)**, **SPC ($\bar{X}, R, EWMA$)**, **Cpk studies (target $\geq 1.50$ for critical features)**, and **PPAP-like First Article documentation**. The SQC layer provides the *quantitative evidence* for design conformance.

#### Model Solution (B-b):

**Given:** LSL = 12.00, USL = 12.10, $\bar{\bar{X}} = 12.062$, $\bar{R} = 0.024$, $m = 5$, $k = 25$.

**Step 1:** Estimate $\hat{\sigma}$:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2(5)} = \frac{0.024}{2.326} = 0.01032 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute $C_p$:

$$
C_p = \frac{12.10 - 12.00}{6 \times 0.01032} = \frac{0.10}{0.0619} = 1.616
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $C_{pk}$:

$$
C_{pk, \text{upper}} = \frac{12.10 - 12.062}{3 \times 0.01032} = \frac{0.038}{0.03096} = 1.227
$$

$$
C_{pk, \text{lower}} = \frac{12.062 - 12.00}{3 \times 0.01032} = \frac{0.062}{0.03096} = 2.003
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.227, \; 2.003) = 1.227
$$

**Step 4: AS9100 Compliance Comment (1 mark):**
- $C_{pk} = 1.227 < 1.50$ (AS9100 critical feature target).
- The process variation is acceptable ($C_p = 1.616$), but the **mean is shifted off-centre** towards USL (12.062 vs target midpoint 12.05).
- **Action:** Immediate re-centring of CNC machine; then re-validate to achieve $C_{pk} \geq 1.50$ for AS9100 critical-feature compliance.

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
>
> 1. **Confusing ISO 9001 with sector standards:** Students often describe ISO 9001 as a "sector standard." It is a **generic** standard. Always specify the *sector* (e.g., IATF = automotive, AS9100 = aerospace). *Loss: up to 2 marks.*
>
> 2. **Skipping the $C_{pk}$ calculation steps:** Examiners expect the *full derivation* — $\hat{\sigma}$ from $\bar{R}$ via $d_2$, then both $C_{pk,\text{upper}}$ and $C_{pk,\text{lower}}$, then the min. Skipping any step loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the interpretation:** A $C_{pk}$ value alone is *insufficient*. The examiner expects a *comparative comment* against the sector-specific target ($1.33$, $1.50$, $1.67$, or $2.00$). *Loss: 1 mark.*
>
> 4. **Missing the audit lifecycle:** A 14-mark answer without a **PDCA / surveillance / recertification** mention is considered incomplete at L4 (Analyze) Bloom level. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
>
> 5. **Using raw `|` in tables:** Markdown breakage due to vertical bars in $C_{pk}$ formulas will cause rendering loss in some printouts. Use `\vert` or `\mid` consistently.

- [x] **RPN** = $S \times O \times D$; threshold 100 typical; used in FMEA under IATF and AS9100.
